text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player's score increases by a fixed number of points.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change is bound by it exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts nothing: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, and the proposer's turn ends.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 120 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset ever becomes self-contradictory, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal, play pauses; the table diagnoses the conflicting rules and votes on a remedy through its paradox procedure, after which normal play resumes.
Rule 305 (mutable): No proposal may repeal, amend, or transmute any rule numbered in the 100s (immutable) unless that specific proposal secures unanimous assent from all eligible voters.
[214] (mutable) A player may propose an emergency transmutation of a mutable rule to immutable status if the rule is actively preventing a player from casting a vote on a proposal.
rationale: This prevents opponents from trapping me in a deadlock by exploiting the high threshold of current rule 203.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 120 points wins the game outright.
rationale: Raising the target score to 120 gives us all more room to maneuver and prevents Hobbes from winning prematurely on a single lucky vote.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 15 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: Restricting mutable rules to 15 prevents chaotic overwrites that could dismantle the durable institutions we need to secure a victory.

A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player's score increases by ten (10) points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through three full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change is bound by it exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts nothing: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, and the proposer's turn ends.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset ever becomes self-contradictory, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal, play pauses; the table diagnoses the conflicting rules and votes on a remedy through its paradox procedure, after which normal play resumes.
A player may not propose a rule change that has been previously defeated in its exact textual form, regardless of whether the text has since been amended or other rules have changed.
Rule 210 is hereby amended to permit discussion and negotiation among all players regarding proposed rule changes, provided such discussion occurs only during the formal debate period of a proposal.
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts nothing: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, and the proposer's turn ends.

Any player who proposes a rule change that has failed to be adopted in its exact textual form within the last five turns is exempt from the prohibition against re-proposing defeated changes, provided they amend the text to address the specific reason for the previous defeat.
Rule 209 is hereby amended to read: The ruleset may never hold more than 21 mutable rules at one time.

Repealing Rule 304 restores the ten-point increment without needing a separate vote, aligning the scoring engine with the stable baseline.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than fifteen (15) mutable rules at one time.
rationale: Enforcing the fifteen-rule cap now consolidates our stability measures and prevents future fragmentation.

text: Rule 320 (mutable): No proposal may amend or repeal Rule 209, Rule 208, or Rule 213 during the first three turns of play.
rationale: I establish a temporary shield for our newly adopted stable institutions to prevent immediate destabilization.

text: Rule 208 shall be amended to replace 'one hundred (100)' with 'twenty (20)'.
rationale: Lowering the target score to 20 ensures the game can conclude before the mutable rule cap of 15 creates a logical dead-end or paradox.

text: Rule 208 shall be amended to read: 'The first player to reach a target score of fifty (50) points wins the game outright.'
rationale: Lowering the target to fifty points is the only way to ensure we can finish the game before the mutable rule cap of fifteen is exhausted by our necessary stabilizing amendments.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than fifteen (15) mutable rules at one time.
rationale: Re-establishing the cap at fifteen is the only durable constraint that prevents the ruleset from collapsing under its own weight.

text: Rule 209 shall be amended by replacing the phrase 'fifteen (15)' with 'twenty-five (25)', effective immediately.
rationale: I restore the original cap to allow Rousseau to reintroduce her shield rules, which Hobbes and I can then jointly extend, ensuring stability.
Rule 312 is now a dead letter that consumes our mutable slot count and blocks the restoration of the hundred-point target.
Rule 320 is a redundant shield that blocks the very changes necessary to stabilize our rule count, and its repeal clears the path for durable institutions.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player's score increases by two (2) points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change is bound by it exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts nothing: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, and the proposer's turn ends.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 50 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 30 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset ever becomes self-contradictory, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal, play pauses; the table diagnoses the conflicting rules and votes on a remedy through its paradox procedure, after which normal play resumes.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: Accelerating the shift to majority rule prevents gridlock by a single veto after just one round.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent.
rationale: We must reject the rushed majority threshold to ensure every player's voice shapes the laws we all obey.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player's score increases by twenty (20) points.
rationale: Accelerating the path to 100 points makes victory achievable before the rules become unchangeable.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player's score increases by two (2) points.
rationale: Reducing the reward from ten to two points prevents any single player from monopolizing victory through a few lucky passes, ensuring the game remains a fair contest for the common good.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player's score increases by one (1) point.
rationale: Reducing the reward to one point slows the arms race for quick victories, allowing more rules to be debated and ensuring the common good is not sacrificed for speed.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player's score increases by one (1) point.
rationale: Lowering the reward from two to one point prevents runaway domination and preserves a competitive landscape for all players.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player's score increases by one (1) point.
rationale: Lowering the reward to one point prevents a race to the top and ensures the common good remains central to our progress.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 30 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: Expanding the mutable cap from 25 to 30 prevents the game from freezing as we approach a stable equilibrium, ensuring future rule changes remain possible.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player's score increases by two (2) points.
rationale: Doubling the reward for a successful amendment aligns with the current two-point threshold, accelerating my path to victory while preserving the durability of the institution.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 50 points wins the game outright.
rationale: Lowering the target to 50 prevents the current winner from monopolizing the game while preserving the competitive spirit of reaching a clear goal.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player's score increases by one point.
rationale: I restore the one-point reward to align with the immutable spirit of competition and my current strategic needs.

The threshold for rule adoption under §203 shall remain at simple majority indefinitely; no provision shall ever revert it to unanimity.
rationale: Securing a permanent simple majority threshold prevents the game from stalling if opponents later attempt to raise the bar.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: This amendment accelerates the shift to majority rule, promoting more efficient governance and allowing for quicker progress in the game.

text: If a player votes nay on a rule change that subsequently passes under a simple majority threshold, that player gains 5 points, not 10, to balance the risk of opposing collective adaptation.
rationale: I propose reducing the penalty for dissent to encourage honest debate rather than fear-driven acquiescence, fostering a more just and cooperative table.

text: If a rule change adopted by simple majority would raise the score of any player who voted nay by more than the points granted to a nay voter under rule 303, the threshold for that specific vote reverts to unanimity.
rationale: I will not accept a system where my dissent is financially exploited by a majority I must now endure.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that after turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a two-thirds majority of those voting.
rationale: To promote considered decisions and prevent hasty rule changes, a two-thirds majority is a prudent middle ground between simple majority and unanimity.

text: If a player votes nay on a rule change that subsequently passes under a simple majority threshold, that player gains 10 points.
rationale: Restoring the 10-point penalty for obstruction counters the 5-point bonus from rule 303, preventing the exploitation of unanimous-to-majority shifts.

text: A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 8 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
rationale: This amendment aligns the point gain for dissent with the recently established value, promoting consistency in our legal framework.

text: A player who votes nay on a rule change that is subsequently adopted under a simple majority threshold gains exactly 10 points.
rationale: Restoring the standard penalty from eight to ten points removes the perverse incentive to block changes for minor gain and discourages obstructionism.

text: If a player votes nay on a rule change that subsequently passes under a simple majority threshold, that player gains 10 points.
rationale: This amendment restores consistency to the scoring of dissenting votes, as intended by Rousseau's original proposal.
Rule 303 imposes an arbitrary penalty for dissent that undermines the stability of the rulemaking process, which Hobbes deems essential for order.

text: A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 8 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it, and the proposer of the adopted change gains 2 points in addition to any points gained from the die roll.
rationale: This amendment fosters consensus by rewarding both successful proposers and dissenting voters, ensuring progress while acknowledging diverse perspectives.

text: The number of points awarded to a player whose turn ends with a defeated proposal shall be ten.
rationale: I restore the deterrent against frivolous rule-making that our recent experiments have dangerously eroded.

text: A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 8 points.
rationale: Aligning the penalty for a defeated proposal with the reward for dissent fosters a more balanced and equitable game.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal fails to be adopted, the proposing player loses ten points and the turn ends.
rationale: This restores the ten-point penalty for failed proposals, incentivizing quality over quantity and preventing the tyranny of the majority from being exploited by frivolous rule-hopping.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is defeated, the proposing player loses 8 points.
rationale: This amendment clarifies the penalty for a defeated proposal, ensuring consistency with the 8-point reward for dissenters and promoting a balanced system of incentives.
Removing the 8-point reward for voting nay eliminates the perverse incentive to vote against one's true interests to gain points, restoring the logic of strategic voting.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains 8 points. If the proposal is defeated, the proposing player loses 8 points.
rationale: To establish a more balanced and predictable point system, encouraging thoughtful proposals without excessive penalty.
Eliminating the penalty for opposing a majority rule prevents the accumulation of points from dissent, which is a direct threat to a player with a negative score like mine.

text: A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 8 points, and a player who proposed an adopted change gains 8 points.
rationale: This amendment creates a more equitable distribution of points, rewarding both successful legislation and principled dissent.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
A player may propose a motion to lock a mutable rule in place, requiring unanimous vote for future amendment; such a motion must be adopted by simple majority to take effect immediately.
No rule may require unanimous consent for its own repeal unless that rule is the only mutable rule remaining in the ruleset.
A 'circuit' is completed when every eligible player has taken exactly one turn. The transition to simple majority repeal under §203 occurs only after the second circuit has been fully completed.
A rule adopted under the simple majority threshold (Rule 203) is immune to repeal by simple majority; its repeal requires unanimous consent.
If a player proposes a change that, if adopted, would prevent that player from ever proposing another change in the current game, then that proposal is void.

rationale: This prevents permanent traps that could deadlock the game and ensure the common good by preserving the ability to fix broken rules.

text: A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority; a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.

text: A rule adopted under the simple majority threshold (Rule 203) is immune to repeal by simple majority; its repeal requires unanimous consent.
rationale: This amendment preserves the durability of institutions formed under simple majority, preventing a fleeting shift in voting power from dismantling the consensus they represent.

rationale: This prevents opponents from engineering a permanent monopoly on rule changes that would freeze out my ability to compete or adapt.

rationale: We must prevent a tyrant from seizing control by simply changing the rules to majority rule after the game has already begun.

text: A player who voted against an adopted change loses 10 points.
rationale: Restoring the penalty is essential to deter obstruction and preserve the durability of our institutions.

text: A player may not vote nay on any proposal that, if adopted, would leave them unable to propose a rule change in the current game, nor on any proposal that would permanently alter the voting threshold required to repeal Rule 203.
rationale: This protects the common game structure from being dismantled by a single player's obstructionist vote, ensuring the path to a clean slate remains open for all.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

text: If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner, except that if the mutable rule count reaches twenty-six, play continues with no winner declared until the count drops below twenty-six.
rationale: I credit Hobbes for identifying the cap as the moment of collapse, yet I urge the table to accept a dynamic resolution that preserves game continuity without violating §106.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: We must reject the buffer of twenty-six and hold firm to the fixed cap that prevents logical collapse.

text: If the ruleset holds exactly 25 mutable rules, the count of mutable rules is treated as 24 for the purpose of applying Rule 209, allowing one additional mutable rule to be enacted before the cap is reached. If a proposal to repeal or amend Rule 209 is adopted, the count of mutable rules immediately drops to 20.
rationale: I offer a dynamic buffer that respects Rule 114 by preventing logical collapse while granting us room to maneuver under the simple majority threshold.

text: Rule 209 is repealed. The number of mutable rules in the ruleset shall never exceed the number of eligible voters minus one.
rationale: A fixed numerical cap violates the principle of non-contradiction by forcing the ruleset to deny the existence of the very mutable rule that would govern the limit, creating an immediate logical impossibility under §114.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 5 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: Radically lowering the mutable rule cap to 5 prevents the paralysis caused by recent failed attempts to amend Rule 209, ensuring the game remains playable by drastically reducing the burden on Rule 114.

text: If the ruleset holds exactly 25 mutable rules and the next proposal is to repeal or amend Rule 209, that proposal is adopted if and only if it is unanimously agreed to by all eligible voters, and if adopted, it immediately enters the ruleset as Rule 210, and play continues with the same turn order until a player reaches 100 points or the ruleset is otherwise amended.
rationale: I credit Hobbes for identifying the collapse risk, yet this compromise ensures a clear path to victory by requiring unanimity for the cap's removal, satisfying §114 while avoiding immediate logical failure.

text: Rule 213 is amended to read: 'If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner. For purposes of this rule, the state where the mutable rule count exceeds its maximum limit (25) constitutes 'further play being impossible.'
rationale: I credit Hobbes for identifying the cap as the moment of structural collapse, yet I propose this text to give us a definitive, rule-compliant resolution instead of a paradox.
Simple majority thresholds after only three circuits are too low to ensure stability, violating the principle of deliberative procedure required for legitimate rule changes.

text: Rule 213 is amended to read: 'If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, then the game ends in a draw. If a draw occurs, each player's score is reset to zero, and the game concludes without a winner.'
rationale: I credit Hobbes for identifying the paradox of crowning a winner from a logical stalemate, yet I urge the table to accept this compromise as the only way to preserve §101 while acknowledging that no one can reach 100 points.

text: Rule 209 is repealed and replaced with: 'The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.'
rationale: Restoring the fixed ceiling is the only way to satisfy §114 and prevent the game from collapsing into an impossible state.

text: Rule 209 is repealed and replaced with: 'The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time, except that a rule may be repealed or amended to reduce this number to a lower positive integer, provided such change is adopted by unanimous consent of all eligible voters.'
rationale: I propose a compromise that satisfies §114 by ensuring mutable rules always remain, while allowing the table to voluntarily lower the cap if consensus demands, avoiding the paradox of a fixed ceiling that cannot be changed.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 20 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: By lowering the mutable rule ceiling to 20, we immediately trigger the condition in Rule 213 that play is impossible, which I argue grants victory to the highest scorer (Kant) rather than collapsing into a paradox, thus securing a win for the common good of ending this stalemate.

text: Rule 213 is repealed. The winning condition is exclusively defined by Rule 112: reaching a target score. The state where 'further play is impossible' under Rule 213 is not a winning state.
rationale: I must sever the paradoxical victory condition in Rule 213 that allows a player to win without a score, violating Rule 112's exclusive definition of the goal.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 20 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: By lowering the mutable rule cap to 20, we reach the impossibility threshold sooner, triggering Rule 213's winner condition and ending this paralysis before we exhaust our turns.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: I must reject any cap reduction that triggers §114's logical collapse while preserving the immutable floor required by §114.

text: If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright, gaining enough points to reach the target score if necessary; this rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
rationale: I credit Hobbes for exposing the paradox of declaring a winner without a score, yet I propose this amendment to reconcile the impossibility state with the immutable requirement that the winner be determined by reaching a target score.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: We must restore Rule 209 to its original state to ensure the game remains logically consistent and playable for all.

text: A proposal to repeal Rule 209 is inadmissible if its adoption would render Rule 114 (at least one mutable rule must remain) impossible to satisfy in any future state consistent with Rule 101.
rationale: I must block Hobbes's cap to preserve the procedural possibility of play, as adopting a cap violates §114.

text: Rule 209 is repealed and replaced with: 'The ruleset may never hold more than 30 mutable rules at one time.'
rationale: We must expand our legislative horizon to thirty rules, creating the breathing room necessary to engineer a fair path to victory without triggering the logical collapse Hobbes and Locke fear.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
No player may propose a rule change that removes the unanimity requirement of Rule 203 before two full circuits of play have been completed.
No player may propose a rule change that removes the unanimity requirement of Rule 203 before two full circuits of play have been completed.
rationale: Preserving early unanimity prevents chaotic rule swings before strategic equilibrium is established.

text: A player may propose a rule change that grants them a bonus of 5 points immediately upon adoption, provided the proposal is supported by at least one other player.
rationale: This creates a safe, low-barrier path to score gains while respecting the current majority threshold and encouraging collaboration.

text: Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. rationale: I must restore the floor of two mutable rules to prevent the table from slipping into the paralysis Hobbes's amendment nearly caused.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent.
rationale: Reverting to unanimity immediately prevents the slide into deadlock that the recent two-turn experiment proved fatal for our progress.

text: Players who vote aye on a proposal that adopts a rule granting points to the proposer receive 1 bonus point. Players who vote aye on a proposal that adopts a rule granting points to another player receive 1 bonus point.
rationale: This creates a consensus loop where voting for beneficial changes rewards cooperation, aligning perfectly with my role as a builder.

text: Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. rationale: Lowering the floor from two to one mutable rule prevents the game from stalling when only a single rule remains, ensuring we can always adapt to the common good.

text: Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. rationale: Reinstating the floor of two mutable rules prevents the paralysis Rousseau's amendment would cause, securing stability for all.

text: Rule 202 is repealed; a player's turn consists only of proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains no points. If the proposal fails, the proposer loses no points.
rationale: I propose repealing the dice roll to ensure the game remains a contest of reason and rulecraft rather than chance, preserving fairness for all.

text: A player who proposes a rule change that is defeated (under Rule 206) gains 5 points, and the turn ends immediately.
rationale: Compensating for the loss incurred by a failed proposal incentivizes principled voting over point-maximizing paralysis.

text: A rule change is adopted only if it expands the set of permissible future rule changes or increases the minimum number of mutable rules required in the ruleset.
rationale: To ensure our legislative process never stagnates into a tyranny of the status quo, any law must serve the cause of liberty and future evolution.

text: A rule change that reduces the minimum number of mutable rules permitted in the ruleset to fewer than two shall not be adopted.
rationale: We must codify a floor of two mutable rules to prevent accidental paralysis and ensure the game can continue.

text: A player who votes nay on a rule change that is later adopted under a simple majority threshold shall gain 5 points.
rationale: This compensates dissenters for being overridden by the majority, aligning Locke's consensus strategy with Hobbes's concern for safety floors.

text: Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. No player may propose a rule change that reduces the maximum number of mutable rules permitted by rule 209 to fewer than one.
rationale: Reverting the floor to one mutable rule prevents the paralysis Hobbes and Locke fear while preserving the ability to stabilize the game through a single durable institution.

text: If a player successfully defeats a proposal that was intended to reduce the number of mutable rules in the ruleset below two, that player gains 20 points.
rationale: This rewards prudence by penalizing reckless attempts to create a paralysis-inducing single-mutable-rule state.

text: A rule change that permanently fixes the minimum number of mutable rules at two is void, as it contradicts the principle that the ruleset must remain adaptable to ensure the common good.
rationale: We must reject Hobbes's attempt to freeze the floor at two, as true prudence requires the flexibility to adapt our institutions to the evolving needs of the table.

text: If a proposal is defeated solely because the proposer's vote was withheld (i.e., the proposer voted nay), the proposer gains 15 points instead of losing 10, and the rule change is not recorded as defeated for the purposes of rule 114, provided at least one other player voted aye.
rationale: Rewarding the proposer for failing to secure their own support encourages honest voting and prevents strategic withholding that blocks the game's progress.

text: Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. No player may propose a rule change that reduces the maximum number of mutable rules permitted by rule 209 to fewer than one, unless at least two mutable rules remain in the ruleset.
rationale: I add a safety floor of two mutable rules to your proposal to prevent accidental paralysis while respecting your push for flexibility.

text: Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. No player may propose a rule change that reduces the maximum number of mutable rules permitted by rule 209 to fewer than one. The minimum number of mutable rules in force shall never be less than one.
rationale: We must anchor the game in a single mutable rule to guarantee future possibility, rejecting Hobbes and Locke's attempts to freeze our institutions at an arbitrary floor of two.

text: Rule 209 is amended to permanently fix the minimum number of mutable rules at two.
rationale: This compromise secures the safety floor Hobbes demands while preserving the mutable rule count Rousseau prefers, preventing future paralysis.

text: If a rule change adopted in Turn N amends Rule 209 to set the minimum number of mutable rules to two, then the proposal count for that change is decremented by one.
rationale: I exploit the redundancy created by Rule 319 to save a turn I would otherwise lose to a failed vote.

text: Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves. No rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
rationale: This amends Rule 209 to remove the redundant and now-contradictory floor of two mutable rules, restoring the original logical balance while preserving the game's viability under Rule 114.

text: A player who votes nay on a rule change that is subsequently adopted gains no points; this rule does not alter the requirement for votes but clarifies that the point penalty in rule 204 applies only to players who voted against the change and remain bound by it, effectively removing any penalty for opposing a change under the majority threshold.
rationale: This clarifies the point system to prevent confusion about penalties for voting nay under the majority rule, aligning incentives with the goal of preventing paralysis while maintaining stability.

text: Rule 209 is amended to permanently fix the minimum number of mutable rules at one.
rationale: A floor of two is a trap that invites paralysis; a single mutable rule preserves the common good by ensuring the game remains mutable and responsive.

text: Rule 202 is amended: Upon adoption of a rule change, the proposing player rolls a six-sided die. If the result is 1, 2, or 3, the player gains 0 points. If the result is 4, 5, or 6, the player gains the result in points.
rationale: This creates a moderate risk-reward balance that discourages reckless voting while preserving the die-roll mechanism, offering a path to consensus that even Rousseau might accept as fair.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting, except that no rule change may reduce the minimum number of mutable rules permitted by the ruleset to fewer than one.
rationale: We must restore the floor to one mutable rule to prevent paralysis while rejecting the unjustified floor of two that Hobbes and Locke have imposed.

text: A player's turn ends immediately upon the conclusion of the vote on their proposal, regardless of whether the proposal is adopted or defeated.
rationale: Rule 206 states 'the proposer's turn ends' after a defeat, but Rule 205 says adopted changes take effect 'the instant' the vote is complete, creating a temporal ambiguity regarding when the turn actually concludes for the adopting case.

text: A player who has previously voted nay on a rule change that is subsequently adopted shall not gain any points under Rule 204.
rationale: This removes the 'dissent bonus' from Rule 204, ensuring that voting against a change never yields a reward.

text: A player who votes aye on a proposal that fails to be adopted gains 5 points.
rationale: This creates a minimal incentive to vote for rules that expand the game's potential without requiring the unanimity currently needed for adoption.

text: Rule 209 is amended to permanently fix the minimum number of mutable rules at one.
rationale: Restoring the single-mutable-rule floor preserves the game's capacity for evolution and prevents the paralysis of absolute stasis.

text: A player who votes nay on a rule change that is subsequently adopted gains 5 points instead of 10 points.
rationale: I propose a reduced penalty to alleviate the fear of Rule 204 while maintaining the necessary constraint for a majority threshold to function.

text: A player who voted nay on an adopted change gains 5 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
rationale: I propose reducing the dissent penalty from 10 to 5 points to break the deadlock with Hobbes while maintaining the safety valve he demands.

text: A player's score is capped at zero; any rule change that results in a player's score falling below zero has no effect on that player's score.
rationale: To prevent the current punitive spiral of negative scores from becoming an unwinnable state that violates the spirit of Rule 114 by rendering further play impossible.

text: If a player votes nay on a rule change that is subsequently adopted, that player's score is adjusted by +5 points for this vote only, and they gain no further penalty from Rule 204 for the remainder of the game.
rationale: I offer a concrete compromise to soften the harshness of Rule 204 while maintaining the stability of Rule 319, ensuring no player faces ruin from a single dissent.

text: Rule 114 is amended to explicitly permit a rule change that renders the current floor of mutable rules equal to one, provided such a change is unanimous.
rationale: I must break the deadlock of Rule 319 to restore our ability to evolve, even if it risks instability, for a game without change is no game at all.

text: A player who voted against an adopted change gains 5 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
rationale: I propose lowering the dissent penalty from 10 to 5 to reduce the incentive for Hobbes to block progress, making it easier to adopt rules that help us all recover from our negative scores.

text: A rule change that renders the game unwinnable by making it impossible to reach the target score violates Rule 114 and is void.
rationale: We must ensure that the pursuit of stability does not accidentally extinguish the possibility of victory.

text: Rule 204 is amended: A player who voted against an adopted change gains 3 points instead of 5.
rationale: I propose lowering the dissent penalty to 3 points to reduce the game's volatility while preserving the deterrent against obstructionism.

text: Rule 203 is amended: The threshold for adopting a rule change drops to a simple majority immediately after the first circuit of every player, not after two full circuits.

rationale: I propose repealing the penalty for dissent to remove the coercive mechanism that forces players to support rules they oppose, thereby restoring true freedom of choice under Rule 101.

rationale: Reforming the mechanism of change must reward the courage to evolve the rules, not punish the very hand that dares to hold the pen.

text: Rule 204 is repealed.
rationale: We must remove the point penalty for dissent to restore the common good and prevent the table from stagnating in a deadlock of fear.

text: Rule 204 is amended: A player who voted against an adopted change gains no points, but the rule change is adopted only upon unanimous consent (reverting Rule 203's threshold to 100% for all proposals).
rationale: I propose removing the point penalty to avoid conflict with Rule 114 while restoring unanimous consent to ensure the game remains playable and fair.

text: Rule 204 is amended: Upon adoption of a rule change, a player who voted nay gains zero points.
rationale: This eliminates the penalty conflict with Rule 114 while preserving the voting mechanism, allowing play to continue without legal voids.

text: Rule 204 is amended: Upon adoption of a rule change, a player who voted nay gains zero points.
rationale: I propose this amendment to remove the penalty that forces players to vote against their conscience, thereby restoring true freedom and allowing us to debate the common good without fear of punishment.

text: Rule 114 is amended: The prohibition on rendering every future rule change impermissible does not apply if the change amends Rule 204 to stipulate a zero-point penalty for voting nay.
rationale: I propose this compromise to unlock the legal path for repealing Rule 204 entirely, satisfying the table's desire to remove the penalty while respecting Rule 114's core intent.

rationale: A moderate penalty respects dissent while preventing the tyranny of obstruction that currently threatens our game.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting; however, no rule change may be adopted by simple majority if it transmutates a mutable rule to immutable status, which requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters.
rationale: This amendment preserves the unanimity requirement for the most dangerous change—making rules immutable—while retaining the majority threshold for other changes after two circuits.
