This transmutation will allow us to adjust rule 103 to better balance the game.

Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
A rule change is adopted when four-fifths of eligible voters vote aye within the first four full circuits of every player, after which the voting threshold shifts to a simple majority.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 120 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
A player may choose to pass their turn instead of proposing a rule change. If they do, they lose 5 points but no rule change is proposed or voted on.
After four full circuits of every player, the voting threshold shifts to a simple majority.
A player may choose to pass their turn instead of proposing a rule change. If they do, they lose 5 points but no rule change is proposed or voted on.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. Players earn a bonus point if their proposal includes a mechanism for transparent, accountable governance that can adapt to unforeseen challenges. Proposals that explicitly address and resolve previous conflicts or disputes in rule application receive a bonus point. To promote durable institutions, players earn a bonus point if their proposal includes a review by a rotating committee every 10 turns to assess the impact of rule changes on the game's stability. An additional bonus point is awarded if the proposal explicitly references and integrates feedback from the last three proposals. Proposals must include a clause allowing for the simplification of rule language to enhance clarity and accessibility. Moreover, proposals must include a clear plan for evaluating the long-term impact of changes, ensuring they are beneficial and sustainable. Proposals must not exceed 500 words to ensure clarity and conciseness. The proposer must ensure that the proposal does not repeat any defeated proposal verbatim within the last three turns. Proposals must include a collaborative clause, encouraging players to engage in pre-debate discussions aimed at refining and enhancing proposals collaboratively, with a bonus point awarded for demonstrated synergy in proposal development. Additionally, proposals must include a method for periodic review by an independent committee to ensure they remain relevant and effective. Proposals must include a mechanism for integrating insights from external experts, with a bonus point awarded for effectively incorporating diverse perspectives. Proposals must include a clause to ensure that no rule change can indefinitely prevent the possibility of any player winning, safeguarding the game's integrity and competitiveness. Furthermore, to encourage innovative and diverse proposals, a proposal that introduces a novel mechanism or concept, not previously included in the last five turns, will be awarded a bonus point. To further foster collaborative efforts, proposals must include a clause for mandatory feedback sessions post-debate, allowing players to reflect on the proposal process and suggest improvements for future proposals.
A rule change is adopted when a majority of eligible voters vote aye, except that in the first circuit, the threshold remains unanimous consent, and in the second circuit, the threshold is 3-2.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 3 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 20 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.

If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution or the proposer of the newer rule wins in the case of a tie.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
The ruleset may never hold more than 20 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: Reducing the mutable limit enhances rule stability, aligning with my preference for durable institutions.

rationale: This amendment allows more flexible rule adoption early on, easing the initial unanimous consent requirement.

If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, with an additional point if the change increases the ruleset's mutability or decreases the number of immutable rules. Players earn a bonus point if their proposal amends an existing rule to improve clarity or efficiency and includes a review clause for future assessment of the change's impact. Proposals that introduce a mechanism for periodic assessment and potential adjustment of existing rules receive a bonus point. A proposal that includes a clause for automatic reassessment of its effects after a certain number of turns receives a bonus point. Players who propose rules that enhance long-term stability by incorporating feedback mechanisms into their proposals receive a bonus point. Additionally, players who propose rules that include a clear rationale for the change receive an extra bonus point. Each player also gains a bonus point if the proposal explicitly outlines how it contributes to durable institutions.
rationale: Encourages proposals that enhance rule stability and institutional durability.

Additionally, to prevent redundancy and foster innovation, any proposal that repeats the exact text of a proposal from the last five turns without substantial modification will be automatically disqualified from receiving bonus points.
rationale: This amendment encourages innovative proposals while preventing redundancy.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. Players earn a bonus point if their proposal includes a mechanism for transparent, accountable governance that can adapt to unforeseen challenges. Proposals that explicitly address and resolve previous conflicts or disputes in rule application receive a bonus point. To promote durable institutions, players earn a bonus point if their proposal includes a review by a rotating committee every 10 turns to assess the impact of rule changes on the game's stability. An additional bonus point is awarded if the proposal explicitly references and integrates feedback from the last three proposals. Proposals must include a clause allowing for the simplification of rule language to enhance clarity and accessibility. text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a ten-sided die twice, adds the results together, and gains that many points. If the sum is more than 15, the player gains only 15 points.

A rule change is adopted when a simple majority of eligible voters vote aye, except that once turn order has carried play through three full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.

Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal. Once turn 32 is reached, the lowest scoring player's vote counts double, and from turn 33 onward, the lowest scoring player's vote counts triple. Once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority of those voting (rule 203), the lowest scoring player's vote counts quadruple from turn 32 onward.

The first player to reach a target score of 87 points wins the game outright; however, this target score is subject to a review vote after every full circuit of play, during which it may be adjusted by at most 5 points up or down.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
The ruleset may never hold more than 22 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: Provides more flexibility for strategic rule changes.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless two full circuits of every player have passed, in which case the threshold drops to a simple majority of those voting, and this threshold shift is permanent unless a subsequent rule explicitly overrides this clause, provided that such overriding rule pertains solely to the adoption threshold mechanism, or if a proposal is explicitly designated as a meta-procedural rule, and no player may successfully propose a rule change to modify this clause unless the current voting threshold is already a simple majority, provided that such modification proposal requires a supermajority of three-quarters of all eligible voters.
rationale: This amendment ensures procedural stability by requiring a higher consensus for altering the voting threshold once it has shifted.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, or if the proposal is a repeal of an existing rule, the proposing player gains 3 points instead.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting, provided that the rule change being voted on does not attempt to amend rule 203, rule 204, rule 208, rule 330, or rule 212; however, amendments to rule 208, rule 212, or rule 330 may be adopted by a simple majority once the threshold has shifted.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.

The ruleset may never hold more than 30 mutable rules at one time, and any rule change that would increase the number of mutable rules beyond 30 must be accompanied by the repeal of an existing mutable rule, unless the rule change is an amendment to an existing mutable rule.

Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves, and players may engage in such discussion if it is part of a formal debate following a rule proposal.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned. The outcome of such a vote is final and cannot be further disputed.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

A player who is currently in last place may, once per circuit of play, propose a rule change that is adopted by a simple majority of all eligible voters, even if the threshold has not yet shifted under rule 203.

A rule change is adopted only when a simple majority of those voting vote aye, except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold remains a simple majority.
rationale: To ensure the stability of the game by establishing a consistent, functional voting threshold from the outset.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: I propose a compromise that shortens the unanimity period to one circuit, balancing stability with the need for progress.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: Reducing the unanimity period to a single circuit provides a reasonable balance between initial stability and the prevention of permanent deadlock.

text: A player may propose a rule change that is specifically intended to repeal or amend an existing rule, provided the proposal is clearly labeled as such in its text.
rationale: This clarifies the intent of proposals to ensure procedural transparency.

text: A player may propose a rule change that is specifically intended to be enacted by a simple majority rather than unanimous consent, provided they include a clause in the text specifying the required voting threshold for that specific rule.
rationale: This provides a structured mechanism for progressive threshold shifts without the blunt instrument of amending the foundational rule 203.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting, provided that the rule change being voted on does not attempt to amend rule 203 or rule 204.
rationale: To ensure the stability of the voting threshold, we must prevent a simple majority from voting to alter the very mechanism that grants them power.

text: Players take a fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. The turn order is: Leibniz, Descartes, Berkeley, Pascal, Malebranche.
rationale: Formalizing the current turn order into the ruleset provides clarity and prevents future disputes regarding the sequence of play.

text: A player may propose a rule change that is specifically intended to increase the target score defined in rule 208.
rationale: As scores rise, increasing the target score may prevent the game from ending too abruptly and ensure a more robust legislative period.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, unless the proposal is an amendment to rule 208, in which case the player gains no points.
rationale: I propose this to prevent players from incentivizing purely self-serving amendments to the winning condition through point-gain mechanics.

text: A player may propose a rule change that is specifically intended to change the target score of rule 208, and such a proposal requires only a simple majority to pass once the threshold in rule 203 has shifted.
rationale: We need a mechanism to adjust the winning condition without the impossible hurdle of unanimity, ensuring the game can adapt to its own duration.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting, provided that the rule change being voted on does not attempt to amend rule 203, rule 204, or rule 208.
rationale: This amendment addresses the concerns raised in previous turns by exempting the winning condition from the unanimity requirement, thereby preventing a single player from indefinitely blocking the conclusion of the game.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting, provided that the rule change being voted on does not attempt to amend rule 203, rule 204, or rule 208; however, amendments to rule 208 may be adopted by a simple majority once the threshold has shifted.
rationale: I propose a compromise that honors the consensus to protect the voting threshold while finally addressing Berkeley's previous suggestion to exempt the winning condition from the unanimity requirement.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 50 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: Increasing the cap on mutable rules prevents the game from becoming prematurely stagnant or legally brittle.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: I am restoring rule 209 to its original text to prevent the potential for excessive ruleset bloat and to maintain procedural clarity.

text: A player may propose a rule change that is specifically intended to introduce a new currency or a new method of scoring, provided such a change is adopted by a unanimous vote of all eligible voters.
rationale: We must create a high-threshold mechanism for fundamental economic shifts to prevent volatile scoring changes from destabilizing the game's long-term equilibrium.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 30 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: A slight expansion of the limit provides necessary legislative breathing room while maintaining institutional restraint.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 30 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: I propose this compromise between the current cap of 25 and Berkeley's previous attempt at 50, aiming to provide the legislative breathing room the assembly desires while maintaining necessary restraint.

text: A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends.
rationale: Reducing the penalty for failed proposals will encourage more diverse experimentation and prevent players from falling into insurmountable point deficits.

text: A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points.
rationale: A 10-point penalty for failure is disproportionately punitive compared to the potential 6-point gain for success, incentivizing reckless rule-making.

text: A player may propose a rule change that is specifically intended to create a new mechanism for scoring, such as a secondary currency or a way to earn points through non-legislative actions, provided that such a rule does not amend rule 208.
rationale: We have refined our legislative procedures; now we must expand our economic possibilities to prevent stagnation.

text: Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. If a player's score reaches a value that is a multiple of 10, they may choose to immediately gain or lose 1 point, provided this choice is made before the next player's turn begins.
rationale: Introducing a minor, controlled mechanism for score adjustment can facilitate the movement of players through the lower scores and provide a measure of agency within the existing structure.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, or if the proposal is a repeal, gains 2 points.
rationale: I propose rewarding the legislative act of repealing rules with a smaller but guaranteed point gain to encourage the cleaning of the ruleset.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal is a repeal, the proposing player gains 3 points instead of throwing a die.
rationale: To encourage the removal of obsolete rules by providing a predictable, low-variance reward for repeals.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points; if the proposal is a repeal, the proposer gains 3 points instead of rolling.
rationale: Providing a fixed reward for repeals incentivizes legislative cleanup and prevents the stagnation of a ruleset cluttered with obsolete provisions.

text: Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries weight proportional to their current score relative to the total sum of all players' scores (where a score of zero or less is treated as having a weight of one).
rationale: To ensure that those who have invested more in the stability of the game through successful rule-making have a greater stake in its future direction.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 40 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: A modest increase to the mutable rule cap allows for necessary procedural complexity while maintaining institutional restraint.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 30 mutable rules at one time, provided that any enactment of a new mutable rule is accompanied by the repeal of an existing mutable rule.
rationale: I propose synthesizing Pascal's 'one-in, one-out' principle with our current cap of 30 to ensure legislative stability.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points; if the proposal is a repeal of an existing rule, the proposing player gains 3 points instead of throwing the die.
rationale: Encouraging the repeal of obsolete rules provides a stable, predictable reward that prevents the inflation of scores while maintaining legislative dynamism.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 30 mutable rules at one time, and any rule change that would increase the number of mutable rules beyond 30 must be accompanied by the repeal of an existing mutable rule.
rationale: This codifies the 'one-in, one-out' principle to maintain the dynamic equilibrium established by our recent consensus.

text: A player who is currently in last place may, once per circuit of play, propose a rule change that is adopted by a simple majority of all eligible voters, even if the threshold has not yet shifted under rule 203.
rationale: To ensure that those most disadvantaged by the current state of the game have a mechanism to catalyze reform.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, unless the proposal is a repeal of an existing mutable rule, in which case the proposing player gains 3 points instead.
rationale: This amendment establishes a measured incentive for the repeal of obsolete rules, promoting the dynamic equilibrium we have collectively sought.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting, provided that the rule change being voted on does not attempt to amend rule 203, rule 204, rule 208, or rule 330; however, amendments to rule 208 or rule 330 may be adopted by a simple majority once the threshold has shifted.
rationale: I propose expanding the exceptions for rule 203 to include rule 330, ensuring that the last-place privilege remains stable while allowing for its eventual adjustment via majority.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 150 points wins the game outright.
rationale: Increasing the target score prevents a premature victory and encourages more meaningful legislative engagement.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 125 points wins the game outright.
rationale: A target of 125 points provides a more balanced duration for play than 100 or 150.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 110 points wins the game outright.
rationale: A target of 110 offers a more balanced progression than 100 or 125, rewarding consistent play without prematurely ending the legislative evolution.

text: Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves, and players may engage in such discussion if it is part of a formal debate following a rule proposal.
rationale: The current restriction on discussion is overly broad and potentially conflicts with the necessity of the debate mandated by rule 211.

text: Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. At the start of each new circuit of play, the players may vote to re-establish a new turn order, provided the vote is unanimous.
rationale: To prevent stagnation, I propose a mechanism to refresh the turn order through unanimous consensus at the start of each circuit.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 30 mutable rules at one time, and any rule change that would increase the number of mutable rules beyond 30 must be accompanied by the repeal of an existing mutable rule, unless the rule being added is a rule numbered in the 300s.
rationale: This allows for necessary procedural rules in the 300s to be added without forcing the repeal of existing core mechanics.

text: Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned. The outcome of such a vote is final and cannot be further disputed.
rationale: I propose adding a finality clause to rule 212 to prevent infinite procedural loops in the event of recurring disputes.

text: A player's score is calculated as the sum of all points gained from successful rule changes and any points gained through penalties applied to others.
rationale: We must formalize the mechanism of point acquisition to ensure the scoring system remains transparent and robust as the game evolves.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
rationale: Returning the target score to 100 restores a standard of competition that is neither too brief nor too protracted.

text: A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 2 points.
rationale: Reducing the penalty for failed proposals encourages more ambitious legislative experimentation by lowering the cost of error.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, or if the proposal is a repeal of an existing rule, the proposing player gains 3 points instead.
rationale: Providing a fixed reward for repeals encourages the necessary pruning of the ruleset to maintain the dynamic equilibrium we have established.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting, provided that the rule change being voted on does not attempt to amend rule 203, rule 204, rule 208, rule 330, or rule 212; however, amendments to rule 208, rule 212, or rule 330 may be adopted by a simple majority once the threshold has shifted.
rationale: I propose extending the simple majority exception to rule 212 to ensure that the finality of the appeal procedure can be refined without requiring unanimity.

text: A player may propose a rule change that is adopted by a simple majority of all eligible voters if they are currently in last place, provided that the rule change does not attempt to amend rule 203, rule 204, rule 208, rule 330, or rule 212.
rationale: This provides a safety valve for the player in last place to influence the ruleset without disrupting the core procedural protections already established.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 30 mutable rules at one time, and any rule change that would increase the number of mutable rules beyond 30 must be accompanied by the repeal of an existing mutable rule, unless the rule change is an amendment to an existing mutable rule.
rationale: Amending a rule should not be penalized by the strict 'one-in, one-out' requirement, as it preserves the existing institutional structure rather than expanding it.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, or if the proposal is a repeal of an existing rule, the proposing player gains 5 points instead.
rationale: Increasing the reward for repealing rules incentivizes players to prune the ruleset and maintain the equilibrium required by rule 209.

text: Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. The turn order is determined by the order of players' names in alphabetical order.
rationale: Establishing a clear, objective method for turn order prevents future disputes and ensures procedural stability.

text: Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. At the completion of each full circuit of play, the turn order is reset to follow the descending order of players' scores at that moment, with the player in last place moving first.
rationale: To ensure the turn order reflects the current state of play and provides a procedural mechanism for movement without the instability of constant amendment.

text: Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. After one full circuit of every player, the turn order is reset to be the descending order of players' current scores, with ties broken by the original turn order.
rationale: A single reset after the first circuit provides a dynamic yet stable mechanism to prevent stagnation while rewarding progress.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
A rule change is adopted when a two-thirds majority of eligible voters vote 'aye' for the first 194 turns, then a simple majority.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

New Rule 301: Players may discuss the game and negotiate about it with one another outside the normal course of play, provided that any agreements reached are subject to the approval of the majority of eligible voters.

New Rule 401: Players may form alliances, with a maximum of two players per alliance. Alliances may propose rule changes together, with their votes counting as a single vote in favor or against the proposal.

New Rule 402: Players may discuss and negotiate about the game outside the normal course of play, provided that any agreements reached are subject to the approval of the majority of eligible voters. This rule is mutable.
New Rule 403: In the event of a tied vote, the proposal fails and no points are gained or lost by the proposer.

A rule change is adopted when every eligible voter votes aye until turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, after which the threshold drops to a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: To encourage exploration of rule changes early in the game while maintaining a majority vote for stability later on.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

rationale: Make Rule 110 mutable to better align mutable and immutable rules

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless the rule change is a transmutation, in which case it requires unanimous consent from all eligible voters.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains 5 points, and every other player who voted aye gains 1 point.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); every player remains bound by the adopted change exactly as if they had voted for it, regardless of how they cast their vote.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, and the proposer's turn ends.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, which must be explicitly cast as either aye or nay, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 50 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through an appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all eligible voters decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, and the proposer's turn ends.
rationale: Removing the severe ten-point penalty for defeated proposals protects all players during this initial phase of unanimous voting and encourages measured, good-faith legislative experimentation.

text: Players may freely discuss the game and negotiate with one another at any time, both during and outside the normal course of play.
rationale: Removing the restriction on communication allows us all to openly coordinate and build consensus on future proposals.

text: Players may freely discuss the game and negotiate with one another publicly at any time, but private communications and secret alliances are strictly prohibited to ensure a fair and transparent discourse.
rationale: By permitting open public discussion while prohibiting secret alliances, we foster cooperative deliberation and ensure fairness for all players without the risk of exclusionary factions.

text: A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); every player remains bound by the adopted change exactly as if they had voted for it, regardless of how they cast their vote.
rationale: The arbitrary ten-point reward for dissenting votes creates a perverse incentive that distorts democratic deliberation and threatens the integrity of our voting process.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains 5 points, and every other player who voted aye gains 1 point.
rationale: By replacing the arbitrary randomness of a die roll with a fixed reward that also compensates assenting voters, we structurally incentivize the drafting of mutually beneficial legislation and ensure all participants have a fair stake in our shared progress.

text: Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play.
rationale: Removing the undefined 'teammates' exception simplifies our framework and ensures that all negotiations occur publicly and transparently.

text: Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through an appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all eligible voters decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
rationale: Building on Nozick's excellent cleanup work, this amendment aligns our dispute resolution rule with Rule 105 by replacing the undefined term 'seated players' with 'eligible voters'.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 50 points wins the game outright.
rationale: Lowering the winning threshold to 50 points ensures the game reaches a satisfying conclusion in a reasonable timeframe given our new fixed scoring system.

text: Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, which must be explicitly cast as either aye or nay, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
rationale: To ensure procedural certainty and prevent legislative gridlock from ambiguous ballots, we must explicitly restrict valid votes to strictly 'aye' or 'nay'.

text: There exists a currency called Influence. Every player's Influence balance begins at zero. Whenever a player's proposal is adopted, that player gains 1 Influence. A player may transfer any amount of their Influence to another player by public declaration.
rationale: Introducing a simple, symmetric currency lays the groundwork for a more dynamic and cooperative legislative economy.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

Once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold for adoption becomes a simple majority of those voting, unless a rule explicitly provides otherwise, provided further that no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

text: A rule change is adopted only when at least two-thirds of the eligible voters assent, except that a rule change transmuting the mutability of another rule requires unanimous assent, and a rule change repealing or amending a mutable rule requires at least two-thirds assent; however, a rule change repealing or amending an immutable rule requires unanimous assent; further, a rule change repealing or amending a mutable rule may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority.
rationale: To balance the need for stability with the necessity for adaptation, allowing for majority rule for mutable rule changes while retaining unanimity for immutables and transmutations.

text: A rule change is adopted when at least one-half of the eligible voters assent, except that a rule change transmuting the mutability of another rule requires unanimous assent, and a rule change repealing or amending a mutable rule requires at least one-half assent; however, a rule change repealing or amending an immutable rule requires unanimous assent.
rationale: To allow for necessary changes without requiring overwhelming consensus, fostering a more dynamic and adaptable game.

rationale: To clarify the distinction between amending mutable and immutable rules, ensuring consistency with the established principles of rule change governance.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
After every 18 turns, the target score for winning the game increases by 11 points.
After every 20 turns, the target score for winning the game increases by 10 points.
After every 25 turns, the target score for winning the game is increased by 12 points.
After every 19 turns, the target score for winning the game is increased by 11 points.
After every 50 turns, the target score for winning the game is increased by 50.
rationale: To encourage longevity and strategic planning in the game.

A rule change may only be proposed by a player who has not proposed a rule change in the last two consecutive turns.

A player may not propose more than one rule change in any three consecutive turns.
