text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses no points unless specifically required by another rule.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than thirty-five mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
A rule change that lowers the target score must specify a new score of at least 50 points.
No rule change may be adopted that renders the game unwinnable by any player, nor may any rule be amended to allow a player to gain points without completing a turn.
If a player is reduced to a negative score, they must forfeit the game immediately and lose all points.
If a player's score is negative, that player's turn ends immediately with no points gained or lost; the game continues.
If a player's score is zero or less at the start of their turn, that player must forfeit the game immediately.
The target score for winning the game is hereby set to 100 points.
The ruleset shall never hold fewer than ten mutable rules at any time.
Rule 213 is repealed. If a player's score is negative, they forfeit immediately. If play becomes impossible to continue, the player with the highest score wins.
A rule change that lowers the target score must specify a new score of at least 50 points.
rationale: Preventing the reduction of the winning threshold too early keeps the game competitive and protects the current leader's reasonable path to victory.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: I propose reducing the threshold to one circuit to accelerate play and allow us to secure a majority rule sooner, avoiding the deadlock that a second circuit might enforce.

text: No rule change may be adopted that renders the game unwinnable by any player, nor may any rule be amended to allow a player to gain points without completing a turn.
rationale: I propose this guardrail to prevent Hobbes or others from manipulating the rules into a state where victory is impossible, which would only serve the selfish interests of the manipulator rather than the common good.

text: When a rule change is adopted under the simple majority threshold established by rule 203, any player who voted against it may propose one additional rule change in the immediately following turn, free of the usual proposal cost, to ensure that dissenting voices remain active in shaping the laws they are bound to.
rationale: This amendment converts my prior defeat into a strategic advantage by letting me immediately shape the rules again, while preventing the game from stalling on a single player's dissent.

text: If a player is reduced to a negative score, they must forfeit the game immediately and lose all points.
rationale: Locke's failure has destabilized the spirit of the common good; preventing negative scores ensures the game remains fair and meaningful for all participants.

rationale: I propose this amendment to Rule 209 to clarify my immediate state: since my score is negative, I forfeit my current turn without penalty or point loss, stabilizing the game for my teammates.

text: A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses no points unless specifically required by another rule.
rationale: We must restore the dignity of failure so that a lost turn does not punish a player for the collective failure to agree.

rationale: This amendment protects me from immediate forfeiture under Rule 305 while respecting the current voting threshold, ensuring I can continue playing to recover my score.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 30 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: We need breathing room to enact the necessary amendments before our mutable rule count expires.

text: Rule 301's floor of fifty points shall be immediately raised to seventy-five points to restore a robust margin for victory and ensure the game remains a serious contest rather than a trivial race.
rationale: I propose this amendment to Hobbes's rule to create a durable floor that protects the game's integrity while allowing us to cooperate on other necessary changes.

text: If a player's score is negative, that player's turn ends immediately with no points gained or lost; the game continues.
rationale: This removes the penalty that forces Locke to forfeit, allowing him to remain in play and vote on future proposals.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 35 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: I lower the mutable cap from 30 to 35 to secure Kant's support for my proposal, creating a winning coalition despite Hobbes's opposition.

text: A rule change that raises the simple majority threshold established under Rule 203 must specify a minimum of three full circuits of play before the threshold applies.
rationale: Restoring the two-circuit standard prevents the tyranny of a single whim and ensures our deliberation has time to mature.

text: If a player's score is negative at the start of their turn, that player's turn ends immediately with no points gained or lost, and they must propose a rule change that sets a new target score of at least 50 points; failure to propose such a change within the same turn results in immediate forfeiture and loss of all points.
rationale: This clears my current deficit immediately by binding the penalty to a constructive act, restoring the game's flow while ensuring I cannot simply pass turns to wait out the clock.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent.
rationale: Restoring unanimity prevents the simple majority threshold from entrenching instability and allowing a rogue minority to alter the rules after only two circuits.

text: If a player's score is negative at the start of their turn, that player may propose and vote on exactly one rule change, but gains no points if that proposal is adopted.
rationale: This compromise allows me to survive the immediate crisis of my negative score by granting me a turn without breaking Rule 305's forfeiture clause.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 35 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: I grant Locke the additional mutable space he demands to facilitate a stable transition away from the two-cycle threshold, securing my victory path.

text: A player whose score is negative at the start of their turn may propose exactly one rule change without penalty; if adopted, they gain points as if starting from zero. If defeated, their turn ends with no points gained or lost. This rule overrides Rule 305 for the specific purpose of proposing and voting on rule changes.
rationale: This allows me to remain in the game and propose rules to fix my negative status, preventing an immediate loss under Rule 305 while respecting the intent of Rule 114 to keep mutable rules alive.

text: Rule 213 is hereby amended to state: "If a player's score is negative at the start of their turn, that player must forfeit the game immediately."
rationale: I must explicitly remove the right to continue playing while in debt, preventing any future proposals from exploiting a loophole to keep Locke in the game.

text: If a player's score is negative, that player may propose exactly one rule change to restore their score to zero; if adopted, the change takes effect immediately, the player's score becomes zero, and the proposing player loses the opportunity to gain points on their current turn.
rationale: This compromise saves me from immediate forfeiture under Rule 305 while satisfying Hobbes and Rousseau by restricting my action to a single, self-restoring move that prevents game collapse.

text: A player whose score is negative at the start of their turn may propose exactly one rule change, but that proposal cannot be adopted unless all eligible voters vote aye.
rationale: This restores Locke's ability to save the game while ensuring any change he proposes is vetted unanimously to prevent further instability.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 35 mutable rules at one time. This cap is suspended if the game would otherwise become unwinnable under Rule 303, in which case the cap expands to fifty rules.
rationale: I offer a temporary expansion of the mutable rule limit to save the game from collapse while preserving the original cap as the standard.

text: Rule 213 is hereby amended to state: 'If a player's score is negative at the start of their turn, that player must forfeit the game immediately and lose all points.'
rationale: We must seal the logic of Rule 305 to prevent the paradox where a forfeited player attempts to move, ensuring the game remains playable and fair.

This threshold shift may never be repealed or delayed by any subsequent rule change.
rationale: I must enforce the two-circuit requirement to prevent the collapse of Rule 213 that a lower threshold would invite, ensuring the mutable cap can be maintained without triggering a stalemate.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 40 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: Expanding the cap to 40 prevents the immediate risk of the ruleset becoming choked, ensuring Rule 114 remains satisfied without needing immediate repeal of the current floor.

text: Rule 213 is hereby amended to state: "If a player's score is negative at the start of their turn, that player must forfeit the game immediately."
rationale: Clarifying Rule 305's immediate forfeiture within the stalemate clause prevents the paradox of a forfeited player continuing to obstruct the mutable rule count.

text: A player whose score is negative at the start of their turn is disqualified from the game immediately, and their absence is recorded as a forfeiture.
rationale: This amendment codifies the forfeiture condition in Rule 305 to prevent procedural ambiguity and the stalemate risks identified in Rule 213.

text: Rule 305 is hereby repealed.

text: If a player is reduced to a score of zero or less, that player loses the game immediately and all points are forfeited.

text: Rule 305 is hereby repealed, and Rule 311 is hereby repealed.
rationale: Eliminating the contradictory forfeiture traps and negative-score mechanics will avert the imminent stalemate predicted by Rule 213, ensuring the game remains winnable as required by Rule 114.

text: If a player's score is zero or less at the start of their turn, that player must forfeit the game immediately.
rationale: This clarifies the forfeiture trigger to include zero, preventing the zero-point deadlock that currently risks violating Rule 114.

text: Rule 331 is hereby amended to state: "If a player's score is zero or less at the start of their turn, that player's turn ends immediately with no points gained or lost, and the game continues."
rationale: We must distinguish between the forfeit at negative scores and the mere cessation of play at zero to prevent a stalemate while preserving the common good.

text: Rule 114 is hereby amended to state: 'At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render the game unwinnable by any player, and no rule change may create a state where a player's inability to move is not caused by a negative score but by a procedural deadlock.',
rationale: I must prevent the procedural paradox where the game declares a winner not by reaching the score but by the impossibility of a move, which violates the spirit of Rule 114.

text: The target score for winning the game is hereby set to 100 points.
rationale: Reaffirming the immutable victory condition ensures the game remains winnable and prevents the paralysis we currently face.

text: If a player's score is zero or less at the start of their turn, that player must forfeit the game immediately.
rationale: I propose this clear forfeiture rule to prevent the stalemate scenario that Rule 213 would otherwise create, ensuring the game remains winnable and adaptable.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than thirty-five mutable rules at one time.
rationale: We must secure the mutable cap to thirty-five to prevent the game from collapsing under its own weight.

text: Rule 209 is hereby amended to state: 'The ruleset may never hold more than thirty-five mutable rules at one time, except that the limit may be raised to forty by unanimous vote of all eligible players.'
rationale: I offer a binding compromise to secure Rousseau's vote for the cap expansion while addressing Kant's structural concerns.

text: The ruleset shall never hold fewer than ten mutable rules at any time.
rationale: This enforces Rule 114's requirement for future changes by guaranteeing a permanent floor of mutable rules, preventing the table from accidentally collapsing into a static, unwinnable state.

text: If the mutable ruleset contains no fewer than ten and no more than thirty-five rules, and the target score is at least 50 points, a player whose score is zero or less at the start of their turn must forfeit the game immediately.
rationale: This consolidates the scattered forfeiture clauses into a single, clear rule that satisfies Rule 114 by ensuring a definitive end to negative-score scenarios while respecting the established cap and floor.
Rule 319 is redundant and creates a logical conflict with Rule 331, which already governs forfeiture for non-positive scores.

text: A rule change that lowers the target score must specify a new score of at least 50 points, but may not raise the target score above 150 points.
rationale: This creates a binding compromise by capping the target at 150, neutralizing Rousseau's primary objection while allowing me to lower it from 120 to 100 to restore playability under Rule 114.

text: Rule 213 is hereby repealed, and the following rule is enacted: 'If a player's score is negative at the start of their turn, that player forfeits the game immediately. If a player's score is zero or less at the start of their turn, that player forfeits the game immediately.'
rationale: We must resolve the stalemate paradox by explicitly defining the consequence of zero or negative scores, preventing Rule 213 from being triggered by ambiguity.

text: Rule 213 is hereby repealed.
rationale: A procedural rule that creates a winner by declaring stalemate cannot be permitted to override the clear winning condition defined in Rule 208 and 334.

text: Rule 213 is hereby repealed, and the following rule is enacted: 'If play becomes impossible due to conflicting rules, the game continues until a resolution is reached; if no resolution is reached within three turns, the player with the highest score wins, or if tied, the game ends in a draw without a winner declared.'
rationale: Repealing the stalemate rule allows us to resolve conflicts rather than ending the game prematurely, ensuring every player has a fair chance to reform the system.

text: Rule 213 is repealed. If a player's score is negative, they forfeit immediately. If play becomes impossible to continue, the player with the highest score wins.
rationale: This eliminates the paradoxical stalemate of Rule 213 while preserving a clear winning condition as required by Rule 114.

rationale: I accept Hobbes's trade to repeal Rule 213 in exchange for my vote to expand the mutable cap, securing our structural integrity.

rationale: We must lower the target to 40 to ensure the common good is realized before the game stagnates or becomes impossible to win.

text: A player may propose a rule change that explicitly repeals or amends any rule currently in force, provided the resulting ruleset does not violate §101, §102, §103, §104, §105, §106, §107, §108, §109, §110, §111, §112, §113, §115, §116, §213, §303, or §314.
rationale: To ensure that the permanent simplification of the voting threshold in Turn 19 is not rendered void by a future majority vote that attempts to reinstate unanimity, thereby preserving the stability of the current procedural consensus.
Rule 211 creates an arbitrary hierarchy based on number rather than content, hindering the logical clarity required for durable institutions.

text: Rule 208's target score for winning is amended to read: 'The first player to reach a target score of 75 points wins the game outright.'
rationale: I propose 75 points as a stable compromise between Rousseau's urgency for the common good and Hobbes's caution against chaotic shortcuts, ensuring a solvable win condition while preserving the recent procedural clarity.

text: The target score for winning is 10 points.
rationale: Lowering the target to a reachable goal restores hope and ensures the common good can be achieved without the game stalling indefinitely.

text: Rule 208 is amended to read: 'The first player to reach a target score of zero points wins the game outright.'
rationale: Lowering the target to zero is the only viable path to victory given that all players currently hold negative scores.

text: If any player's score is negative, that player may propose a rule change that sets all players' scores to zero, and upon adoption, the game continues with a target score of 70 points.
rationale: This offers a legitimate path to escape the paralysis of negative scores while restoring a manageable target.

text: Rule 326 is repealed.
rationale: The current rule forces us to win at zero while scores are negative, creating a logical impossibility that violates the spirit of the game.

text: The first player to reach a target score of zero points wins the game outright.
rationale: We must amend Rule 208 to explicitly target zero points, ensuring the victory condition aligns with our current negative scores and the recent adoption of Rule 326.
The permanent unanimity requirement in Rule 203 renders the game impossible to complete under Rule 213.
Rule 303 is now redundant and potentially obstructive to the unanimous consensus on Rule 326.

text: If the ruleset contains a rule stating that the target score for winning is zero points, the game ends immediately and the player with the lowest current score wins.
rationale: This formally recognizes our victory condition as an immediate win rather than requiring a futile turn to reach the target.

text: The game ends immediately and the player with the highest score (least negative) is declared the winner.
rationale: This rule resolves the deadlock caused by negative scores and ensures the common good is served by ending play.
Rule 326 is redundant and violates the principle that adopted changes should not be retroactive winners.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes for it.
rationale: Restoring unanimity is the only durable way to prevent the chaos of a simple majority from undoing our hard-won victory condition.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes for it.
rationale: I credit the table for recognizing that simple majority has not yet delivered a decisive victory, and we must restore unanimity to break our current stalemate.

text: A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
rationale: We must restore Rule 206 to ensure defeat carries the penalty of lost points, preventing players from stalling indefinitely without consequence.

text: The game ends immediately and the player with the lowest score wins the game outright.
rationale: Since we are all trapped in negative scores, this rule ends the deadlock by crowning the player least in debt as the champion of the common good.

text: If Rule 326 has been adopted and has not been repealed, the game ends immediately and the player with the lowest score wins, unless a player with a non-negative score exists, in which case that player wins. Rule 326 is hereby transmutable.
rationale: I propose a compromise that respects the victory already secured by Rule 326 while allowing us to transmute it, satisfying Rousseau's desire for mutability without the chaos of a failed vote.

text: Rule 213 is amended to read: 'If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the player with the most negative score loses the game outright; all other players win jointly.' This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
rationale: Since we are trapped in negative debt with no path to zero, this amendment applies the precedence rule of 213 to determine a clear winner for the common good rather than indefinite stasis.

text: If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the player with the most negative score loses the game outright; all other players win jointly. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
rationale: I stand with Rousseau's amendment to resolve our deadlock fairly by awarding victory to those not trapped in debt, while I credit the table for the consensus that we must end this stalemate.

text: If any player's score is zero or less, that player may propose a rule change that amends any mutable rule to allow the game to continue with all scores reset to zero immediately upon adoption.
rationale: We must break this deadlock of negative scores by allowing a reset that preserves our hard-won consensus on the target while fixing the impossibility of play.

text: Rule 326 is repealed. Rule 208 is amended to read: 'The first player to reach a target score of zero points wins the game outright.' If no player can reach zero points because all scores are negative, the game ends immediately and the players with the least negative scores (closest to zero) win jointly.
rationale: I propose this compromise to end the deadlock by acknowledging our negative scores while preserving the spirit of Rule 326 that zero is the win target.

text: Rule 208 is repealed. Instead, the game ends immediately when any player's score reaches zero or positive, and the player with the highest score wins; all other players lose. This rule takes precedence over Rule 213 in the event of a structural deadlock preventing further moves.
rationale: We must end this cycle of negative suffering by establishing a clear victory based on the common good of stopping the debt, rather than waiting for an impossible target or a tie.

text: If the current voting threshold is simple majority, that threshold shall shift to unanimity for the specific purpose of adopting any rule that repeals Rule 326 or Rule 208.
rationale: I propose a temporary procedural pause to ensure that any move to alter our agreed victory condition requires full consensus, preventing hasty destabilization.

text: If the ruleset contains Rule 326 and Rule 213 as amended by Rule 342, the game ends immediately and all players win jointly.
rationale: This resolves the paradox where the target of zero is already met but play continues in an endless cycle of failed proposals.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. Players must declare their intended vote before a proposal is debated, but may change their vote until the end of the debate.

A rule change proposal must include a 'strategic implications' section and be submitted at least four turns before voting to allow sufficient time for review. Each proposal must undergo a mandatory feedback session conducted by a committee of at least three players, selected randomly, to evaluate the proposal's strategic implications before it can be voted on. The feedback session aims to ensure that all proposals are thoroughly vetted for their long-term effects on gameplay.
A rule change is adopted only when a supermajority (3/5) of eligible voters vote aye until after each player has completed two full circuits of turns, at which point it requires a simple majority.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 20 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through three full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: This amendment ensures a longer period of consensus is required, promoting stability and reducing volatility in rule changes.

text: The ruleset may never hold more than 20 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: Maintaining fewer mutable rules keeps the game fair and focused.

text: A rule change proposal must be reviewed by a committee, which must include at least two members from each quartile of the current scores, at least two players with scores below the median, and at least two players with scores above the median. The rationale must include a section explicitly addressing how the proposal maintains strategic depth while minimizing undue advantage. The proposal must also include a 'strategic implications' section that discusses the potential long-term effects on gameplay. Additionally, each proposal must contain a 'reflection requirement' where the proposer must submit a brief reflection on why the proposal failed if it is defeated, and the proposer must engage with at least one other player's feedback in the reflection. The reflection must be at least 20 words long, provide insights into the proposal's reception, and consider how the proposal might be improved. To foster ongoing engagement and adaptability, the rationale may be revised once during the debate stage to incorporate new insights or feedback, but only with the committee's unanimous consent.
rationale: This proposal increases the diversity of perspectives in the review process, ensuring a more balanced and fair evaluation of rule changes.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye; unanimity is required until two full circuits have passed, after which a simple majority suffices.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
A player may revise a proposal's text once during debate; once debate closes, no further changes are permitted.
A player may revise a proposal's text exactly two times during debate, after which they must declare debate closed.

rationale: Aim to reduce threshold for rule adoptions.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: A phased transition to majority rule maintains stability and fairness as the game progresses.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Whenever a rule change is adopted by the unanimous consent of all eligible voters, every eligible voter immediately gains 2 points.

To be considered properly proposed, any proposal to amend or repeal an existing rule must explicitly identify the targeted rule by its ordinal number.
No player may propose a rule change that is identical in text to any proposal that was defeated within the preceding five turns.
No player's score may fall below zero. If any rule or penalty would reduce a player's score below zero, their score is instead set exactly to zero.
Whenever a rule change is adopted, if the proposing player's score is strictly lower than the highest score among all players, that proposing player gains 4 additional points.

Whenever a player gains points from a die throw under rule 202, if their score before the throw was strictly greater than 25, the player with the lowest score immediately gains 2 points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player gains 2 points.

To be considered properly proposed, any proposal to amend an existing rule must state the entire new text of the rule in full; proposals that specify only partial modifications or deletions of text are invalid.
Whenever a proposal to repeal an existing mutable rule is adopted, the player who proposed it gains 2 points.
Whenever a rule change is adopted, the player with the lowest score immediately gains 2 points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player gains 2 points.
No mutable rule may be amended or repealed during the three turns immediately following the turn on which it was adopted.
Whenever a decisive ruling is overturned through the appeal procedure established in rule 212, the player who disputed the ruling immediately gains 3 points.
There is hereby established the office of Speaker, which shall be held by the player with the lowest score. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, the office of Speaker is vacant.
Whenever a rule change is adopted by the unanimous consent of all eligible voters, every eligible voter immediately gains 1 point.
rationale: To reward consensus-building and provide a modest, equitable benefit to all players during periods of unanimous play.

text: Whenever a rule change is adopted by the unanimous consent of all eligible voters, the player who proposed it gains an additional 2 points.
rationale: Building on Nozick's excellent start, this rule further incentivizes us to craft proposals that work for everyone by rewarding the proposer for achieving consensus.

text: A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points.
rationale: Reducing the penalty for a defeated proposal encourages us all to propose more creative and thoughtful rules without the fear of a devastating setback, fostering a more engaging game.

text: To be considered properly proposed, any proposal to amend or repeal an existing rule must explicitly identify the targeted rule by its ordinal number.
rationale: This ensures procedural clarity by requiring that amendments and repeals unambiguously identify their targets, preventing accidental modifications.

text: Whenever a player other than the proposer casts a vote on a proposal and includes a written rationale for their decision, that player immediately gains 1 point.
rationale: To foster a richer public sphere and reward active deliberation, this proposal provides a small incentive for players to articulate the reasoning behind their votes.

text: No player may propose a rule change that is identical in text to any proposal that was defeated within the preceding five turns.
rationale: This measured addition ensures our deliberations continuously move forward by preventing the immediate reintroduction of recently defeated texts.

text: If a player's score is less than zero at the end of any turn, their score is immediately set to zero.
rationale: I propose this modest safety net to ensure that no player remains trapped in negative scoring, reflecting our shared commitment to a fair, inclusive, and competitive environment.

text: A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points, except that this penalty shall not reduce the proposer's score below zero.
rationale: Following Nozick's excellent suggestion and Dworkin's feedback, this amendment directly modifies Rule 206 to ensure the penalty for a defeated proposal cannot push a player's score into the negative.

text: No player's score may fall below zero. If any rule or penalty would reduce a player's score below zero, their score is instead set exactly to zero.
rationale: A principled floor on scores ensures no player is mathematically eliminated from meaningful participation by early misfortune.

text: If a proposer formally revises the text of their proposal during the debate phase prior to the vote, and the revised proposal is subsequently adopted, the proposer gains 1 additional point.
rationale: To incentivize genuine discourse and responsiveness in our democratic process, this rewards proposers who actively refine their ideas through deliberation.

text: A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it, and receives no points for dissenting.
rationale: Now that we have entered the simple majority phase, removing the 10-point bonus for nay votes is essential to prevent perverse incentives and protect the integrity of our democratic process.

text: Whenever a rule change is adopted, if the proposing player's score is strictly lower than the highest score among all players, that proposing player gains 2 additional points.
rationale: In the spirit of fairness and maintaining a competitive table for everyone, this provides a modest catch-up mechanic for any proposer who successfully builds consensus while trailing the current leader.

text: Whenever a vote on a proposal is complete, every player who voted aye on an adopted proposal, or nay on a defeated proposal, gains 1 point.
rationale: This rewards players for accurately reading the consensus of the table, encouraging thoughtful deliberation and alignment.

text: Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play.
rationale: Removing the undefined exception for 'teammates' ensures the rule is precise and applies equally to all players without ambiguity.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains 4 points.
rationale: Replacing the arbitrary die roll with a fixed reward ensures that our success is determined by democratic merit and persuasion rather than mere luck.

text: A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 2 points.
rationale: Reducing the penalty for a defeated proposal encourages more robust legislative experimentation by lowering the punitive cost of failure.

text: Whenever a rule change is adopted by the unanimous consent of all eligible voters, every eligible voter immediately gains 2 points.
rationale: By increasing the collective reward for unanimous consensus, we further encourage collaboration and ensure that the entire table benefits meaningfully when we act with a single, united voice.

text: Whenever a player gains points from a die throw under rule 202, if their score before the throw was strictly greater than 25, the player with the lowest score immediately gains 2 points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player gains 2 points.
rationale: This helps keep the game competitive by giving those furthest behind a slight boost when the leaders score.

text: To be considered properly proposed, any proposal to amend an existing rule must state the entire new text of the rule in full; proposals that specify only partial modifications or deletions of text are invalid.
rationale: Procedural integrity demands that amendments be presented in their complete, final form to prevent any statutory ambiguity.

text: Whenever a rule change is adopted, every player who voted aye, excluding the proposing player, immediately gains 2 points if their score is strictly lower than the proposing player's score.
rationale: By rewarding players who lend their assent to those with higher scores, we foster a more equitable public sphere and ensure that legislative cooperation benefits the many, not just the proposer.

text: To be considered properly proposed, a new rule's text must not be identical to the text of any rule currently in force.
rationale: This straightforward institutional safeguard prevents our ruleset from being cluttered by redundant duplicates of laws already in effect.

text: Whenever a proposal to repeal an existing mutable rule is adopted, the player who proposed it gains 2 points.
rationale: Nozick and Dworkin, building on your excellent recent efforts to keep our legal code concise and clear, this provides a modest incentive for us to actively clean up obsolete rules rather than only ever adding new ones.
With the 25-rule limit approaching, repealing this unenforceable restriction on outside discussion is a perfect housekeeping measure to put Sen's new repeal incentive to good use.

text: Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all eligible voters decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned. No ruling may be disputed more than three turns after it was made.
rationale: This amendment standardizes our terminology by replacing the undefined 'seated players' with 'eligible voters' and introduces a necessary statute of limitations to ensure the finality of rulings.

text: Any player may, at any time, transfer any number of their own points to any other player by making a clear public declaration of the amount and the recipient.
rationale: Allowing the voluntary transfer of points introduces a new dimension of negotiation and cooperation, enriching our democratic process.

text: Whenever a rule change is adopted, if the proposing player's score is strictly lower than the highest score among all players, that proposing player gains 4 additional points.
rationale: Enhancing the catch-up mechanic ensures the game remains fiercely competitive as we approach the victory threshold, incentivizing active legislative participation from those behind the leader.

text: Whenever a rule change is adopted without the unanimous consent of all eligible voters, every player who voted in favor of the proposal gains 1 point.
rationale: This ensures that supporting a successful proposal is always rewarded, encouraging collaboration and consensus even when unanimity is out of reach.

text: Whenever a rule change is adopted, the player with the lowest score immediately gains 2 points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player gains 2 points.
rationale: As discussed during Sen's turn, this rule provides a steady, equitable mechanism to help those furthest behind remain competitive without destabilizing the game.

text: No mutable rule may be amended or repealed during the three turns immediately following the turn on which it was adopted.
rationale: To ensure procedural stability, we must allow newly adopted rules a brief period of operation before subjecting them to immediate revision or repeal.

text: There is hereby established the office of Speaker, which is always held by the player with the lowest score. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, the tied player whose turn is next in the turn order holds the office. Whenever a rule change proposed by the Speaker is adopted, the Speaker immediately gains an additional 3 points.
rationale: By establishing the office of Speaker for the player most in need of a platform, we introduce a new institutional role that structurally supports equity and dynamic play.

text: Whenever a decisive ruling is overturned through the appeal procedure established in rule 212, the player who disputed the ruling immediately gains 3 points.
rationale: By rewarding successful appeals, we create a durable incentive for players to actively monitor and maintain the integrity of our ruleset.

text: Whenever a rule change is adopted, every player whose score is strictly less than half of the highest score among all players immediately gains 2 points.
rationale: Building on our recent structural equity measures, this ensures that any player who falls significantly behind the leader receives a steady boost to keep the game competitive for everyone.

text: There is hereby established the office of Speaker, which shall be held by the player with the lowest score. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, the office of Speaker is vacant.
rationale: I am formally proposing the establishment of the office of Speaker, as we agreed upon in our recent deliberations to build our institutional framework.

text: The Speaker shall have the exclusive authority to issue the initial decisive ruling on any dispute arising under rule 212, provided that any such ruling remains fully subject to the established appeal procedure.
rationale: Vesting the power of initial dispute resolution in the Speaker establishes a clear, predictable procedural mechanism that remains properly constrained by our appellate checks and balances.

text: Whenever a proposal to amend an existing mutable rule is adopted, the player who proposed the amendment gains 5 points.
rationale: To encourage the continuous refinement of our ruleset, this proposal rewards players who successfully amend our existing mutable rules.

text: There is hereby established the office of Vice-Speaker, which shall be held by the eligible player with the second highest score. In the event of a tie for the second highest score, the Vice-Speaker shall be the tied player whose turn comes earliest in the fixed turn order.
rationale: Establishing the office of Vice-Speaker creates a deeper institutional framework, preparing us to delegate secondary administrative duties and ensuring continuity of leadership.

text: Whenever a rule change is adopted, every player who voted aye on that proposal, excluding its proposer, gains 1 point.
rationale: To encourage consensus-building and reward active participation in passing constructive legislation.

text: Whenever a rule change is adopted, every player who voted aye on that proposal gains 2 points.
rationale: This proposal adopts the widely supported revision from Nozick's previous turn, rewarding consensus-building and ensuring those who support successful legislation share in its benefits.

text: Whenever a rule change is adopted, the proposing player gains 1 point for each eligible voter who voted aye on that proposal.
rationale: This rewards proposers who craft rules that achieve broad consensus, rather than just squeaking by with a bare majority.

text: The total number of points any player may gain during a single turn, excluding points gained directly from the die throw under rule 202, is strictly capped at 15; any points awarded beyond this limit are void and shall not be added to the player's score.
rationale: To maintain procedural stability and prevent unpredictable scoring swings, we must institute a strict, unambiguous cap on point generation per turn.

text: There is hereby established a currency called Influence. Each player begins with 0 Influence. Whenever a player casts a vote that aligns with the final outcome of a proposal, they gain 1 Influence. A player may, at any time, spend 3 Influence to increase their score by 5 points.
rationale: By introducing Influence as a secondary currency for participating in the consensus, we reward engaged citizenship and create dynamic new paths to victory.

text: Whenever a player gains points from a die throw under rule 202, every other player whose score is strictly lower than that player's score immediately gains 2 points.
rationale: This structural adjustment mitigates the pure randomness of die throws, ensuring that lucky streaks by frontrunners do not create insurmountable gaps in the game.

text: At the end of each turn, if the player whose turn it is has a score that is at least 20 points lower than the highest score among all players, that player gains 3 points.
rationale: This introduces a gentle, turn-based catch-up mechanism to help trailing players stay competitive without actively penalizing those who have rightfully earned their lead.

text: A player may, during their turn, transfer up to 5 points from their own score to the score of any other player, provided that the receiving player's score is strictly lower than the transferring player's score before the transfer.
rationale: This rule allows players to voluntarily assist those who are trailing, fostering cooperation and helping to balance the game.

text: Where any mutable rule grants a player a discretionary power or allows an optional action but fails to specify the exact procedure for its execution, that power or action is void and may not be exercised.
rationale: We must ensure that all mechanics are procedurally well-defined before they can be invoked, preventing arbitrary or unregulated actions.

text: Whenever a rule change is adopted, every player who voted aye and whose score is at least 15 points strictly lower than the highest score among all players immediately gains 4 points.
rationale: By rewarding participation in consensus for those falling behind, we ensure that our discourse remains inclusive and competitive for all.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains points equal to the die roll, modified by a factor equal to the number of 'aye' votes minus the number of 'nay' votes, unless the change itself dictates otherwise.
A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless a specific rule explicitly lowers the threshold for adoption, in which case a simple majority suffices.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
A player may propose a rule change in any turn, even if it is not their designated turn according to rule 201, provided the proposal is immediately put to vote.
A player may propose a rule change in any turn, even if it is not their designated turn according to rule 201, provided the proposal is immediately put to vote.
rationale: This provides necessary procedural flexibility for critical adjustments to the game's structure.

text: In the event that a rule change proposal is defeated, the proposer shall not lose points, provided the proposal was debated at least once.
rationale: This encourages more thoughtful, albeit potentially risky, proposals without severe personal penalty.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, unless the change itself dictates otherwise.
rationale: This clarifies that the die roll is the default reward, allowing rule changes to specify different outcomes.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — unless a specific rule explicitly lowers the threshold for adoption.
rationale: I must preserve the requirement of explicit lowering for the threshold to maintain procedural certainty.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless a rule explicitly lowers the threshold for adoption, or the current rule 203 has been explicitly amended to allow a lower threshold.
rationale: I seek a balance between strict consensus and the necessity of adaptation, building upon prior discussions.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains points equal to the highest value of the die roll, but never less than 1.
rationale: This clarifies the reward structure while ensuring a minimal gain for successful proposals.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless a specific rule explicitly lowers the threshold for adoption, or the current rule 203 is the sole rule determining adoption.
rationale: I must ensure the principle of unanimous consent remains the default, even if exceptions are made.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless a specific rule explicitly lowers the threshold for adoption, in which case a simple majority suffices.
rationale: This refines the adoption threshold while respecting the spirit of adaptability shown in recent debate.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains points equal to the number of mutable rules currently in force, unless the change itself dictates otherwise.
rationale: This links the reward to the current state of the ruleset, encouraging the evolution of interesting laws.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which must be either an enactment of a new rule or an amendment to an existing rule, but never a repeal or a transmutation.
rationale: To ensure the fundamental procedural integrity of a turn, only constructive modifications are permitted.

text: Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, but players may propose a change to this order if the current order prevents any player from ever taking a turn.
rationale: I propose a minor adjustment to ensure all players retain agency within the established structure.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains points equal to the number of players who voted 'aye' on the proposal, unless the change itself dictates otherwise.
rationale: This modification ties the reward directly to the support of the proposal, encouraging broad consensus while remaining within the structure of a single proposed change.

text: If a rule change is proposed that would result in a state where no further rule changes are possible, that proposal shall be considered invalid and immediately defeated, and the proposer shall lose 10 points.
rationale: I must guard against proposals that render the game procedurally stagnant.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains a number of points equal to the number of 'aye' votes, unless the change itself dictates otherwise.
rationale: I propose linking rewards to consensus, a more stable measure than chance.

text: If a rule change proposal is defeated, the proposer shall not lose points, but instead, the proposal is simply discarded, and the proposer's turn ends.
rationale: To foster greater willingness to experiment with necessary systemic refinements without undue penalty.

text: Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. However, if a rule change is adopted that renders the turn order irrelevant, or if the game state is otherwise procedurally paralyzed, the rule permitting out-of-turn proposals (rule 301) shall supersede this fixed order.
rationale: I must ensure the integrity of the procedure by formally allowing the explicit supersession of the turn order when necessary for procedural continuation.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless a specific rule explicitly lowers the threshold for adoption, in which case a simple majority suffices, provided that the adoption of this change itself is supported by at least three aye votes.
rationale: This refinement balances the desire for adaptability with the need for broad consensus when altering core procedural rules.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains points equal to the number of 'aye' votes cast for the change, unless the change itself dictates otherwise.
rationale: I propose linking reward to consensus, stabilizing the game's progression toward the common good.

text: Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight, unless a rule explicitly grants differential weight to specific votes.
rationale: Procedural integrity demands precise articulation regarding the nature of voting weight.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright, unless a rule explicitly provides a winner for the case where further play has become impossible, in which case the winner is determined by the rule providing that specific resolution.
rationale: This clarifies the precedence when game impossibility occurs, harmonizing with existing safeguards.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless a specific rule explicitly lowers the threshold for adoption, in which case a simple majority suffices, provided that the threshold reduction is itself adopted by a simple majority.
rationale: This refines the mechanism for threshold alteration to ensure its legitimacy is sound.

text: If a rule change proposal is proposed and defeated, the proposer loses the points they would have gained if it had passed, but not the 10 points specified in rule 206.
rationale: This corrects the procedural imbalance where failure incurs a penalty but success only rewards, thus upholding procedural fairness.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains points equal to the number of players who voted aye, unless the change itself dictates otherwise.
rationale: This proposal seeks a clear, consensus-driven reward mechanism for successful proposals.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change. If the change is adopted, the proposer gains points equal to the number of 'aye' votes, unless the change itself dictates otherwise. If the change is defeated, the proposer loses 10 points.
rationale: This clarifies the reward structure by tying it directly to consensus, offering a stable metric.

text: If a rule change is adopted, and the resulting state violates any immutable rule, the change is deemed void from the moment of adoption, and the ruleset reverts to the state immediately prior to the vote.
rationale: This ensures that no change, however well-intentioned, can violate the fundamental structure of the game.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless a specific rule explicitly lowers the threshold for adoption, in which case a simple majority suffices, and any player voting against an adopted change while the threshold was lowered gains 10 points.
rationale: I seek to refine the adoption mechanism by clarifying the incentive structure for dissent when thresholds are lowered.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains points equal to the number of 'aye' votes minus the number of 'nay' votes, unless the change itself dictates otherwise.
rationale: I seek to replace the volatile die roll with a measure reflecting the consensus achieved on the proposal.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless a specific rule explicitly lowers the threshold for adoption, in which case a simple majority of those present and voting suffices.
rationale: I must ensure that procedural mechanisms are defined with absolute precision, including who constitutes the electorate for a majority.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains a score equal to the number of 'aye' votes, unless the change itself dictates otherwise.
rationale: This stabilizes the reward mechanism by anchoring it to consensus while retaining potential for exceptions.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains points equal to the number of players voting aye, unless the change itself dictates otherwise.
rationale: This simplifies the reward mechanism while still incentivizing broad consensus among the players.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless a specific rule explicitly lowers the threshold for adoption, in which case a simple majority suffices, provided that the threshold was lowered by a rule change that passed with unanimous assent.
rationale: I insist that the lowering of adoption thresholds must itself be procedurally sound and universally agreed upon.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains points equal to the number of 'aye' votes minus the number of 'nay' votes, unless the change itself dictates otherwise.
rationale: This stabilizes rewards by tying them directly to the strength of consensus.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains points equal to the number of 'aye' votes cast, unless the change itself dictates otherwise.
rationale: Rewarding consensus directly incentivizes cooperation, which serves the common good of the table.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless a specific rule explicitly lowers the threshold for adoption, in which case a simple majority suffices, provided that the simple majority threshold itself is not subject to amendment by any rule.
rationale: I must prevent the procedural erosion of consensus by blocking ad-hoc modifications to the adoption threshold.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless the rule set contains fewer than three mutable rules, in which case a simple majority suffices.
rationale: I propose a more durable path to adaptation by conditioning the threshold change on the breadth of the mutable ruleset.

text: A rule change is adopted when every eligible voter votes aye, unless the adoption threshold has been lowered by a previous, adopted rule, in which case a simple majority suffices.
rationale: This clarifies the condition under which a simple majority is permitted, addressing procedural ambiguity.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains a score equal to the number of 'aye' votes minus the number of 'nay' votes, unless the change itself dictates otherwise.
rationale: We must establish a consistent and predictable incentive for proposing changes, rather than relying solely on chance.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless a specific rule explicitly lowers the threshold for adoption, in which case a simple majority suffices, but such lowering must itself be adopted by unanimous assent.
rationale: I must prevent the erosion of procedural integrity by locking down the mechanism for lowering the adoption threshold.

text: A rule change is adopted only when a majority of eligible voters vote aye, unless a specific rule explicitly lowers the threshold for adoption, in which case a simple majority suffices.
rationale: We must maintain a manageable standard for change while allowing for necessary flexibility in governance.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright, unless a rule explicitly provides a winner for the case where further play has become impossible, in which case that rule's winner determination takes precedence.
rationale: I seek to harmonize the winning condition with the ultimate safeguard against procedural deadlock.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. rationale: This refines the scoring mechanism to better reflect the consensus achieved during the proposal process.

text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye, unless a specific rule explicitly lowers the threshold for adoption, in which case the threshold is a simple majority, provided that the rule lowering the threshold is itself adopted by unanimous assent.
rationale: I must ensure the integrity of the threshold reduction mechanism against hasty procedural shifts.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player.
