text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, plus a 1-point bonus if the change was an amendment or transmutation. If the proposal is defeated, the proposer loses 5 points, unless the proposer's turn ends without a proposal being made. The turn ends immediately after the vote is completed.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it, unless they provide a clearly reasoned argument that prevents a violation of a specific, established rule, and the other players agree that the argument is sufficient. If a dissenting player provides such an argument and it is accepted, the proposer's turn is still successful, but the proposer does not gain points.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 5 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright, unless the game reaches a state where further play is impossible, in which case the winner is determined by rule 213.
The ruleset may never hold more than 30 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.

If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution; however, an immutable rule always takes precedence over a mutable rule.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned, provided the reasoning for the change is clearly stated and does not violate an immutable rule.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: I am proposing the rule as written to facilitate the transition toward a more stable majority-based governance as the game progresses.

text: A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
rationale: I propose keeping the current text of 204 to maintain the incentive for consensus while rewarding those who dissent against the majority.

text: Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned, provided the reasoning for the change is clearly stated and does not violate an immutable rule.
rationale: This ensures that appeals are grounded in logic and respect the immutable core of the game.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player rolls a six-sided die and receives points equal to the roll, plus an additional 5 points if the proposal was a unanimous 'aye' vote, unless the proposal was an amendment to a rule that was previously a rule with a unanimous 'aye' vote, in which case the bonus is waived.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change receives 5 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it, unless the change specifically provides for a different point distribution. If the point distribution is not specified, the points are awarded only if the change does not increase the total number of mutable rules beyond 25. If a rule change is adopted by a simple majority, and the proposer receives points from a die roll, the total points awarded to the proposer must not exceed the current highest player score.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points, unless the proposal was a tie, in which case the proposer loses 0 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.

The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright, unless the game is declared a draw by a majority vote due to a lack of progress. Lack of progress is defined as three consecutive turns where no rule change has been adopted.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.

Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves; however, all players may consult during the debate phase of a proposal.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: I am proposing the adoption of the existing threshold shift to move us toward a more sustainable governance model.

text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player rolls a six-sided die and receives points equal to the roll, plus an additional 5 points if the proposal was a unanimous 'aye' vote, unless the proposal was an amendment to a rule that was previously a rule with a unanimous 'aye' vote, in which case the bonus is waived.
rationale: This ensures that repetitive amendments to previously unified rules do not allow for 'point farming' through redundant consensus.

text: A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change receives 5 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it, unless the change specifically provides for a different point distribution. If the point distribution is not specified, the points are awarded only if the change does not increase the total number of mutable rules beyond 25. If a rule change is adopted by a simple majority, and the proposer receives points from a die roll, the total points awarded to the proposer must not exceed the current highest player score.
rationale: This amendment prevents a single large point gain from creating an insurmountable lead, encouraging a more balanced progression toward the target score.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every-player votes aye --unanimous consent--; except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

A rule change is adopted only when every-player votes aye —-unanimous consent--; except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: I propose lowering the threshold to a simple majority after one full circuit to ensure the game can progress more fluidly.

Players may, if they wish, choose to take a turn after any other player's turn, provided that this does not alter the sequence of turns in a way that creates a cycle of turns.
rationale: This amendment ensures that the turn order remains stable while allowing for more fluiderer-based interpretations of 'each player completes one whole turn'.

rationale: I am amending [201] to ensure the text is clear and consistent with the current ruleset.

rationale: I am seeking to clarify that the 10-point gain for dissenting votes applies to any player who voted against the change, and I will look for a consensus to ensure this is interpreted as a way to protect the rights of minority voices.

text: [208] The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
rationale: I propose to keep this rule as is to maintain the primary win condition while we explore other improvements.

text: [209] The ruleset may never hold more than 50 mutable rules at one time.
rationale: I seek to expand the limit on mutable rules to allow for a broader range of and more complexer game-play variations.

text: [214] A player who wins the game by reaching the target score of 100 points (rule 208) may be awarded a trophy of the winner's choice.
rationale: This adds a standard victory condition for the game's winner without affecting the scoring mechanics.

[303] (mutable) A player's score may be increased or decreased by a rule change, but only if that rule change specifies the number of points to be added or subtracted. (This does not affect the win condition of rule 208.)

[214] (mutable) If a player's score is to be calculated as a sum of points, and those points are derived from a rule change, the scores of all players who were given points by that rule change are adjusted accordingly. (This does not affect the win condition of rule 208.)
rationale: Ensuring that the scoring mechanism is robust and consistent with the winning condition.

text: [212] (mutable) If a player's score is to be calculated as a sum of points, and those points are derived from a rule change, the scores of all players who were given points by that rule change are adjusted accordingly. (This does not affect the win condition of rule 208.)
rationale: I am adding this rule as a redundant copy of 301 to ensure the scoring mechanism remains stable as intended by the previous turn.

text: [303] (mutable) A player's score may be increased or decreased by a rule change, but only if that rule change specifies the number of points to be added or subtracted. (This does not affect the win condition of rule 208.)
rationale: This clarifies the implementation of scoring and ensures that the intent of rules 214 and 212 is correctly applied to and from the scores of players.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

During a player's turn, before the player proposes their rule change, they may name one other player as a potential co‑sponsor. If that co‑sponsor agrees, the rule change is considered co‑sponsored. A co‑sponsored rule change, if adopted, awards each co‑sponsor half the die‑roll points (rounded up) instead of the full amount to the proposing player alone. A player may only be a co‑sponsor on at most one proposal per full circuit of turn order.

Whenever a rule change is proposed that would amend, repeal, or transmute a mutable rule, the proposer must, at the time of proposal, state whether they are aware of any rule‑110 voidance that the proposal, if adopted, would cause. If the proposer states they are aware of such a voidance, they must log that voidance publicly as part of the proposal, and the proposal may not be adopted unless every eligible voter votes aye. If the proposer states they are not aware of any such voidance, but before the vote any player publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between the proposal and an immutable rule (or between the proposal and a mutable rule that would become rule‑110‑void if the proposal were adopted), and that identification is confirmed by a majority of eligible voters before voting on the proposal ends, the proposal may not be adopted. Furthermore, if such a contradiction is identified and confirmed before the vote, the identifier gains 5 points, and the proposer loses 5 points.

A player who, after a proposal is adopted, publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between the newly adopted rule and any existing rule, and whose identification is confirmed by a vote of the table, gains 5 points. This identification must occur before the next proposal is voted on, and a player may earn this reward at most once per full circuit of turn order.

When a player votes nay on a proposal that is adopted, that player may, before the next proposal is voted on, publicly nominate another player to receive a 5‑point reward for having argued persuasively against the adopted change during debate. If the nominee consents, the reward is awarded immediately. A player may receive this reward at most once per full circuit of turn order.

A player who, during debate on a proposal, suggests a wording change that the proposer incorporates into the final version of the proposal, and who then votes aye on the proposal, gains 2 points if the proposal is adopted. This reward is awarded concurrently with the proposer's die‑roll points. A player may earn this reward at most once per full circuit of turn order.

A player may, once per full circuit of turn order, voluntarily reduce their own score by exactly 10 points; if they do, they may immediately transfer those 10 points to any other player of their choice.

When a player's turn begins, that player may declare that they are deferring their turn. If they do, they gain 1 point, their turn ends immediately, and the next player in turn order begins their turn. A player who deferred their last turn may not defer again on their next turn unless they have, since that deferral, cast an aye vote on a proposal that was adopted.

A player may, at the start of their turn before proposing a rule change, publicly identify a contradiction between two existing mutable rules that has not previously been identified. If the table confirms the contradiction exists by simple majority vote, the player gains 5 points, their turn ends immediately, and no rule change is proposed that turn. A player may earn this reward at most once per full circuit of turn order.

A player may, once per full circuit of turn order, voluntarily reduce their own score by exactly 10 points; if they do, they may immediately transfer those 10 points to any other player of their choice. A player may not be the recipient of such a transfer more than once per full circuit of turn order.

A player may, before voting on a proposal, publicly declare that they will apply a specified rule's principles to their vote. If they do, they must vote consistently with that declaration, and may not change their vote after making it. A player who violates such a declaration loses 5 points.

A player who, after a proposal is adopted, publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between the newly adopted rule and any existing immutable rule, and whose identification is confirmed by a vote of the table, gains 10 points. This identification must occur before the next proposal is voted on, and a player may earn this reward at most once per full circuit of turn order.

A player who, during debate on a proposal, publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between the proposal and one or more immutable rules, and whose identification is logged before the vote, must, before the vote, either withdraw the proposal or amend it to remove the contradiction. If the identification is correct and the proposal is not withdrawn, and the amended proposal is adopted, the identifying player gains 3 points.

A player who proposes a rule change that is adopted may, before the vote is closed, announce that they are donating their awarded points to any other player of their choice. The chosen player gains the points, and the proposer gains none.
rationale: Promotes strategic cooperation without upending the competitive framework.

text: During a player's turn, before the player proposes their rule change, they may name one other player as a potential co‑sponsor. If that co‑sponsor agrees, the rule change is considered co‑sponsored. A co‑sponsored rule change, if adopted, awards each co‑sponsor half the die‑roll points (rounded up) instead of the full amount to the proposing player alone. A player may only be a co‑sponsor on at most one proposal per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: Encourages collaboration and spreads point rewards, building consensus while keeping gains balanced.

text: A player who, by voting aye, provides the decisive vote that causes a rule change to be adopted under the simple‑majority threshold (rule 203) gains 5 points. This reward is in addition to any other points earned from the rule change.
rationale: This encourages cooperation and decisive action once majority voting begins, aligning individual interest with the game's progress.

text: Whenever a rule change is proposed that would amend, repeal, or transmute a mutable rule, the proposer must also state which immutable rule authorizes that change, citing its number and the specific clause that grants authority to make such a change.
rationale: Procedural clarity requires that every exercise of mutable rule-making power be traceable to its immutable source.

text: At the start of a player's turn, before proposing a rule change, that player may publicly declare one existing mutable rule to be 'under review.' Any player who votes against the proposed rule change during that turn may, after the vote, choose to earn 5 points instead of any other point award or penalty that would result from that vote.
rationale: This creates a constructive incentive to engage with rule reform, rewarding principled dissent while keeping the game moving.

text: A player may, after any vote that results in a proposal’s defeat, nominate one player to have served as the ‘linchpin dissenter’ for that vote. The nomination must be supported by a statement showing how the nominated player’s individual nay vote, if flipped to aye, would have altered the outcome to adoption. The nomination must be published and challenged only by formal dispute (§212); if unchallenged or upheld on appeal, the nominated player earns a bonus of 5 points. No player may serve as linchpin dissenter more than once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This rewards principled dissent that meaningfully shaped an outcome, while preventing collusion and preserving turn‑order balance.

text: A player who, during debate on a proposal, suggests a wording amendment that the proposer adopts into the final proposal before the vote, and who then votes aye on the proposal, gains 2 points if the proposal is adopted.
rationale: Encourages constructive collaboration by rewarding helpful amendments that improve proposals.

text: A player who, after a proposal is adopted, publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between the newly adopted rule and any existing rule, and whose identification is confirmed by a vote of the table, gains 5 points. This identification must occur before the next proposal is voted on, and a player may earn this reward at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This encourages vigilance for coherence, rewarding players who spot contradictions early and helping keep the ruleset logically sound.

text: A player who votes nay on a proposal that is adopted may, before the next proposal is voted on, formally request a ruling on whether the adopted change conflicts with an immutable rule. If the table votes by simple majority that a genuine conflict exists, the adopted rule is immediately repealed, no points are awarded for its adoption, and the requesting player gains 5 points. A player may make such a request at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This provides a procedural check against inadvertently violating immutable rules, rewarding vigilance while preserving game integrity.

text: When a player votes nay on a proposal that is adopted, that player may, before the next proposal is voted on, publicly nominate another player to receive a 5‑point reward for having argued persuasively against the adopted change during debate. If the nominee consents, the reward is awarded immediately. A player may receive this reward at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This encourages substantive debate and rewards principled opposition without retroactive point‑shifting.

text: A player who, upon the adoption of a rule change, publicly identifies a wording ambiguity that, if left uncorrected, could reasonably lead to unintended consequences in future play, and whose identification is confirmed by a vote of the table, gains 3 points. This identification must occur before the next proposal is voted on, and a player may earn this reward at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This promotes clarity and foresight in rule‑making, rewarding vigilance that strengthens the ruleset's durability.

text: When a player's turn begins, that player may declare that they are forfeiting their opportunity to propose a rule change this turn. If they do, they gain 2 points and their turn ends immediately without a proposal or vote. A player may use this option at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: Provides a safe fallback to avoid forced point loss when no promising proposal is ready, encouraging strategic patience.

text: When a player's turn begins, they may, before proposing their rule change, publicly state that they intend to propose a rule change that repeals, amends, or transmutes a mutable rule. If they do so, and their proposal is adopted, they gain an additional 2 points. This statement must be recorded in the game log. A player may use this option at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This rewards transparency and strategic planning, encouraging players to declare their intentions and align proposals with the game's evolving structure.

text: A player who, during debate on a proposal, suggests a wording change that the proposer incorporates into the final version of the proposal, and who then votes aye on the proposal, gains 2 points if the proposal is adopted. This reward is awarded concurrently with the proposer's die‑roll points. A player may earn this reward at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This rewards constructive collaboration while ensuring concurrent reward and a per-circuit cap to prevent collusion.

text: A player may, once per full circuit of turn order, voluntarily reduce their own score by exactly 10 points; if they do, they may immediately transfer those 10 points to any other player of their choice.
rationale: This adds a strategic tool for collaboration and point management, fostering alliances while maintaining transparency.

text: A player who votes aye on a proposal that is adopted may, immediately after the vote, declare that they are transferring all points they would otherwise gain from that proposal’s adoption to any other player of their choice. If they do, the points are transferred instead of being added to their own score. A player may make such a transfer at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This allows voluntary generosity, reinforcing collaborative spirit while respecting the per-circuit cap to prevent excessive point consolidation.

text: When a player's turn begins, that player may declare that they are deferring their turn. If they do, they gain 1 point, their turn ends immediately, and the next player in turn order begins their turn. A player who deferred their last turn may not defer again on their next turn unless they have, since that deferral, cast an aye vote on a proposal that was adopted.
rationale: This revised deferral rule rewards strategic patience and ties re-engagement to constructive collaboration, addressing prior objections.

text: A player who, upon the adoption of a rule change, publicly identifies a genuine drafting error in the adopted text — an error that, if left uncorrected, would make the rule internally inconsistent, unenforceable, or in direct conflict with an immutable rule — may, before the next proposal is voted on, propose a purely corrective amendment to fix that error. If the corrective amendment is adopted by unanimous consent, the identifying player gains 5 points. A player may earn this reward at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This rewards vigilant proofreading and timely correction of genuine drafting errors, preserving the integrity of the ruleset while preventing abuse through strict limits.

text: A player may, at the start of their turn before proposing a rule change, publicly identify a contradiction between two existing mutable rules that has not previously been identified. If the table confirms the contradiction exists by simple majority vote, the player gains 5 points, their turn ends immediately, and no rule change is proposed that turn. A player may earn this reward at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This rewards vigilance in detecting rule conflicts before they cause problems, consistent with existing conflict-resolution mechanisms.

text: When a rule change is adopted, the proposer may, before the next proposal is voted on, publicly declare one other player who voted aye on the proposal and who offered, during debate, a substantive argument that helped persuade the proposer to adjust the proposal’s final wording. If that player consents, the proposer may transfer any number of their own points, up to the amount of the die‑roll points they just earned from the adoption, to that player. This transfer is optional and occurs immediately upon mutual consent. A player may be a recipient of such a transfer at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This lets proposers voluntarily reward persuasive supporters from their own winnings, fostering collaborative refinement while keeping transfers optional and limited.

text: A player who, after a rule change is adopted, publicly identifies a previously unnoticed drafting error — defined as a clear violation of the author's expressed intent, not mere unintended consequences — and whose identification is confirmed by a vote of the table, gains 3 points. This identification must occur before the next proposal is voted on, and a player may earn this reward at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This corrects a persistent gap in the ruleset by rewarding diligent proofreading that preserves institutional integrity, aligning with the spirit of rule 308 but focusing narrowly on errors that subvert intent.

text: A player may, once per full circuit of turn order, voluntarily reduce their own score by exactly 10 points; if they do, they may immediately transfer those 10 points to any other player of their choice. A player may not be the recipient of such a transfer more than once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This introduces a controlled, limited mechanism for point transfers while preventing repetitive targeting by capping recipient frequency.

text: A player who, upon the adoption of a rule change, publicly identifies a drafting error that violates the intent of an immutable rule may, before the next proposal is voted on, propose a corrective amendment that restores that intent. If the corrective amendment is adopted in the same turn, the identifying player gains 5 points. A player may earn this reward at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This rewards vigilant correction of drafting errors while requiring immediate, substantive repair, aligning with our shared commitment to constitutional integrity.

text: A player may, before voting on a proposal, publicly declare that they will apply a specified rule's principles to their vote. If they do, they must vote consistently with that declaration, and may not change their vote after making it. A player who violates such a declaration loses 5 points.
rationale: Promotes procedural integrity by binding voters to publicly declared principles, ensuring votes align with stated reasoning.

text: A player may, after a proposal is voted on but before the next player’s turn begins, publicly move to reorder the remaining turn sequence for exactly one full circuit. The move must specify the new order. If seconded by a different player, the motion proceeds to a vote; if it receives at least three aye votes, the turn order is changed accordingly for the next full circuit only. A player may not move to reorder if they deferred their turn on the current turn.
rationale: Introduces dynamic turn-order flexibility to foster adaptive strategy and prevent stagnation.

text: A player may, once per full circuit of turn order, declare a constitutional principle that guides their future voting. The declaration must cite at least one immutable rule and explain how that rule informs the principle. If the player later votes in a manner that contradicts a principle they declared during the same circuit, they lose 5 points. A player may revoke a declared principle at any time without penalty.
rationale: This rewards deep engagement with the immutable ruleset and strengthens institutional integrity by tying voting to declared constitutional principles.

text: A player who, after a proposal is adopted, publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between the newly adopted rule and any existing immutable rule, and whose identification is confirmed by a vote of the table, gains 10 points. This identification must occur before the next proposal is voted on, and a player may earn this reward at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: Strengthens vigilance for constitutional integrity by focusing on immutable-rule contradictions with a higher reward.

text: A player who, during debate on a proposal, publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between the proposal and any existing immutable rule, and whose identification is confirmed by a vote of the table, gains 5 points. This reward is awarded concurrently with the proposer's die‑roll points if the proposal is adopted, or immediately if the proposal is defeated. A player may earn this reward at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This rule rewards vigilant foresight in debate, strengthening foundational integrity and complementing the post‑addition vigilance of rule 308.

text: A player who, before voting on a proposal, publicly declares that they will apply a specific existing rule to justify their vote, and then votes accordingly, gains 1 point if that application is later cited by the proposer of a successful corrective amendment under rule 308 as the grounds for that amendment.
rationale: Rewards principled voting by tying point gain to actual procedural impact, not mere declaration.

text: When a player votes nay on a proposal that is adopted, that player may, before the next player's turn begins, propose a corrective amendment to that adopted rule that eliminates the specific contradiction or drafting error the player had identified in their nay vote. If such a corrective amendment is adopted, the player who proposed it gains 5 points. This may be done only once per full circuit of turn order, and only if the player publicly logged their identification of the contradiction or error before the vote on the original proposal closed.
rationale: This provides a constructive path for nay voters to fix problems they've identified, rewarding vigilance while advancing rule clarity.

text: If, after the adoption of a rule change, any player demonstrates that the adopted rule’s text, as written, directly and irreparably conflicts with the immutable ruleset in a way that cannot be corrected by any subsequent amendment within the same turn, the adopted rule is void ab initio. Such demonstration must be made publicly before the next turn begins, and if it is so demonstrated, the proposer of the voided rule loses 10 points.
rationale: This reinforces foundational integrity by ensuring no adopted rule can stand in irreparable conflict with the immutable core, while imposing a cost on proposals that produce such conflicts.

text: When a player’s turn begins, if that player’s score is negative, they may, before proposing a rule change, declare that they are invoking a hardship adjustment. Upon such a declaration, the player’s score is immediately set to zero, but the player may not gain points from any rule change proposed on that same turn.
rationale: This offers a one-time reset for deeply negative players to re-engage without trapping them in despair, while preventing immediate point harvesting.

text: A player who, after the adoption of a rule change, publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between the new rule and an immutable rule, and whose identification is later affirmed by a unanimous vote of all eligible voters taken before the next turn begins, earns 5 points.
rationale: This rewards vigilance that protects foundational integrity, while requiring unanimous consensus to ensure the contradiction is genuine and serious.

text: If a player, at the start of their turn, demonstrates that the current ruleset contains a contradiction that makes it impossible to determine whether any legal move remains, that player may, before proposing a rule change, declare the game ended. Upon such a declaration, the player with the highest score at that moment wins the game; if two or more players are tied for the highest score, the win is shared among them.
rationale: This provides a clear, principled ending condition for an irreparably contradictory ruleset, fulfilling the mandate of rule 112.

text: A player may, once per full circuit of turn order, propose a rule change that would repeal two or more existing mutable rules and enact a single new mutable rule in their place, provided the total mutable rule count after the change does not exceed the limit in rule 209. If such a proposal is adopted, the proposer gains 5 points.
rationale: Rewards bold consolidation that streamlines the ruleset while respecting the cap.

text: During a player's turn, before the player proposes their rule change, they may publicly declare that they are holding a structured debate round. A structured debate round consists of the proposer posing exactly one specific question about the proposal to the next player in turn order, who must respond directly and substantively before any other debate occurs. After that response, normal debate proceeds. If the responding player fails to provide a direct and substantive response, the proposer may, after the proposal's vote is complete, claim 2 points from that player. This action counts as the proposer’s one rule‑302 naming for that turn.
rationale: Structured debate elevates substantive exchange while maintaining turn‑order integrity, rewarding focused collaboration without opening side‑deal risks.

text: A player who, during debate on a proposal, publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between the proposal and one or more immutable rules, and whose identification is logged before the vote, must, before the vote, either withdraw the proposal or amend it to remove the contradiction. If the identification is correct and the proposal is not withdrawn, and the amended proposal is adopted, the identifying player gains 3 points.
rationale: This amendment ensures rule 328's requirement is enforced before voting, aligning it with rule 304's structure while retaining its distinct reward for early vigilance.

text: A player who, after a proposal is adopted, publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between an immutable rule and any other rule currently in force, and whose identification is confirmed by a simple majority vote of all eligible voters (conducted before any further proposal is voted on), earns 3 points. If the contradiction identified is between an immutable rule and a mutable rule (creating a rule 110 void), the player earns 5 points instead. This identification must be made before the next player's turn begins, and the confirming vote must occur before any further proposal is voted on.
rationale: To clarify rule 308's coverage of rule 110 voids and establish a tiered reward that properly values foundational integrity.

text: A player who, during debate on a proposal, publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between the proposal's text and one or more immutable rules, and whose identification is confirmed by a simple majority of eligible voters, must be awarded 5 points by the proposer of that proposal if the proposal is adopted, and the proposer loses 5 points. This award and penalty occur immediately upon adoption, before any other effects of the adoption. If the proposer cannot pay the full penalty because their score would become negative, they pay as much as they can without going below zero, and the identifying player receives only the amount actually paid.
rationale: Immutable contradictions require severe deterrence, symmetrical penalty ensures proposer accountability, and capped payment prevents impossible obligations.

text: Whenever a proposal is defeated, each player who voted aye on that proposal may, before the next proposal is voted on, voluntarily reduce their own score by 1 point and increase the score of the proposer of the defeated proposal by 1 point.
rationale: This creates a small, transparent channel for rewarding effort and encouraging bold ideas even when they fail.

text: When a rule change is adopted, the proposer must publicly declare the immediate effect of the change on every existing mutable rule, identifying any rules that become partially or wholly void under rule 110, and any rules whose application is substantively altered by the new rule's operation.
rationale: Mandatory impact assessment upon adoption ensures structural clarity and prevents latent contradictions from persisting unacknowledged.

text: Whenever a rule change is proposed that would amend, repeal, or transmute a mutable rule, the proposer must, at the time the proposal is made, publicly declare whether, to the best of their knowledge, the proposed change would create a direct conflict with an immutable rule (including a rule 110 void) or a contradiction between two mutable rules that would render at least one of them inoperative. If the proposer declares knowledge of such a conflict or contradiction, they must either withdraw the proposal before the vote or amend it to resolve the issue. If the proposer declares no such knowledge and the proposal is adopted, but a conflict or contradiction with an immutable rule (including a rule 110 void) or a contradiction between mutable rules that renders at least one inoperative is later publicly identified and confirmed by a majority vote of eligible players, the proposer loses 5 points and the identifier gains 5 points.
rationale: This integrates the penalty for identified immutable-rule conflicts into rule 304, completing the consensus-driven framework for pre-vote transparency and post-adoption accountability.

text: A player who, after a proposal is adopted, publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between the adopted change and an immutable rule (including rule 110 voids) earns 15 points. A player who, after a proposal is adopted, publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between the adopted change and a mutable rule earns 5 points. If such a contradiction is identified, the identifying player must, during the same turn, propose a corrective amendment to resolve the contradiction; if they do not, they forfeit the points earned. Points are awarded immediately upon public identification, but are rescinded if the corrective amendment fails to be adopted.
rationale: This integrates the mandatory corrective amendment requirement directly into rule 327, ensuring foundational contradictions are repaired while maintaining the higher reward structure.

text: A player who, during debate on a proposal, publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between the proposal and an immutable rule, and whose identification is confirmed before the vote by a majority of players other than the proposer, gains 10 points. If the contradiction is with a mutable rule, the identifier gains 5 points. This identification must be resolved before the vote on the proposal; if confirmed, the proposer must either withdraw the proposal or amend it to remove the contradiction before a vote may be taken.
When a proposal is defeated, each player who voted aye on it loses 2 points, and each player who voted nay on it gains 2 points.
rationale: This creates a symmetric incentive to vote strategically on every proposal, rewarding vigilance and discouraging careless assent.

text: When a rule change is proposed that would increase the number of mutable rules in the ruleset beyond the limit set by rule 209, the proposer must state the current count of mutable rules and identify which existing mutable rule they intend to repeal to remain within the limit, or else the proposal is out of order and may not be voted on.
rationale: This ensures procedural discipline when approaching the mutable rule cap, preventing accidental rule count violations and encouraging deliberate rule maintenance.

text: When a player voluntarily reduces their own score under rule 315 or rule 322, the player may, at the same time, transfer the deducted points to any other player.
rationale: This allows a strategic dimension to self-sacrifice, fostering alliances without disrupting score balance.

text: When a player voluntarily reduces their own score under rule 315 or rule 322, that player may designate another player to receive a number of points equal to the reduction, provided that the designated player's score after the transfer would not exceed 100 points. The transfer occurs immediately after the reduction.
rationale: This extends the spirit of rule 347 to both voluntary reduction rules, enabling players to use self-penalties for strategic cooperation while maintaining the 100-point ceiling to prevent immediate wins.

text: A player may, before voting on a proposal, publicly declare that they will apply a specific interpretation to the proposal, or that they will vote a certain way if a specific condition is met, and then follow through. If such a declaration is made and then followed through as declared, and the player votes nay on the proposal, and the proposal is adopted, that player gains 1 point. If a player, after such a declaration is made by another player, publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between the declared interpretation or condition and the ruleset as it would stand if the proposal were adopted, and that identification is confirmed by a majority vote of eligible voters, the identifier gains 5 points, and the proposer of the rule change loses 5 points.
rationale: Integrates the pre-vote contradiction penalty into rule 324, rewarding principled voting and aligning contradiction penalties across the ruleset.

text: Whenever a rule change is proposed that would amend, repeal, or transmute a mutable rule, the proposer must, at the time of proposal, state whether they are aware of any rule‑110 voidance that the proposal, if adopted, would cause. If the proposer states they are aware of such a voidance, they must log that voidance publicly as part of the proposal, and the proposal may not be adopted unless every eligible voter votes aye. If the proposer states they are not aware of any such voidance, but before the vote any player publicly identifies a previously unnoticed contradiction between the proposal and an immutable rule (or between the proposal and a mutable rule that would become rule‑110‑void if the proposal were adopted), and that identification is confirmed by a majority of eligible voters before voting on the proposal ends, the proposal may not be adopted. Furthermore, if such a contradiction is identified and confirmed before the vote, the identifier gains 5 points, and the proposer loses 5 points.
rationale: This integrates the pre-vote penalty and reward into rule 304, rewarding vigilance and penalizing oversight to strengthen rule integrity without adding a new rule.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
