rationale: Increasing the target score to 250 points to make the game more challenging and prolong play.

text: (339) (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that modifies the target score only if it is unanimously agreed upon by all eligible voters, ensuring that score adjustments are consensual.
rationale: Ensures that any adjustments to the target score require full consensus, maintaining fairness and balance.

text: (341) (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that decreases the target score by up to 5 points, provided that the new target score remains above the current target score, only if the proposed rule change is unanimously approved by all eligible voters. This rule is intended to adjust the game's difficulty dynamically and prevent the target from becoming unattainable, thus maintaining a balanced and engaging gameplay.
rationale: This amendment allows for flexibility in adjusting the target score dynamically to keep the game challenging yet achievable.

text: 342 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that modifies the voting rule to a three-quarters majority.
rationale: This change allows for more consensus in rule adoption, potentially benefiting all players by requiring broader agreement.

text: 343 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that reduces the target score by up to 20 points, provided the target remains above 100, in order to strategically extend the game duration and promote more intricate play.
rationale: To strategically prolong gameplay and enhance strategic depth by allowing players to adjust the target score, thus preventing premature conclusions and encouraging prolonged tactical planning.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 300 points wins the game outright.
rationale: Raising the target score will extend gameplay and require more strategic rule changes, benefiting all players.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 250 points wins the game outright.
rationale: Maintains the existing target score to prevent further rule changes that could delay the game unnecessarily.

text: (344) (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that allows proposing multiple rule changes in a single turn, provided that each rule change adheres to the current ruleset. Each proposed rule change must be voted on separately.
rationale: This change aims to streamline the rule-changing process by allowing multiple rule changes in one turn while maintaining individual voting for each.

text: (347) (mutable) A player may propose a rule change to adjust the target score by up to 5 points, subject to approval by unanimous consent.
rationale: This change allows for strategic flexibility in adjusting the target score, provided there is unanimous agreement among all players.

text: (348) (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that allows for a temporary suspension of play if a player reaches a negative score of -10 points, with the condition that the game resumes after a minimum of one turn.
rationale: This proposal aims to address the issue of a player reaching a negative score, ensuring the game can continue smoothly without penalizing players excessively.

text: The first player to reach a target score of 200 points wins the game outright.
rationale: Lowering the target score to 200 points reduces the total number of mutable rules, allowing for more flexible rule changes while maintaining a challenging game.

text: (349) (mutable) A player may propose a rule change to adjust the target score by up to 5 points once per game, provided the new target does not exceed 300 points.
rationale: This proposal aims to provide strategic flexibility while maintaining a challenging target.

text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

A player who votes aye on a proposal that is adopted gains 1 point, provided the proposal was not a transmutation and the proposer also gains points from its adoption, and provided further that the voting threshold at the time of the vote was already a simple majority of those voting.

Before a player may propose a rule change, they must publicly declare their intended kind of change (add, amend, repeal, or transmute) and any target rule numbers. This declaration is binding; if the proposal as voted does not match the declaration, the proposal is considered illegal and is discarded without a vote. The proposer's turn ends immediately, and they lose 10 points.
If a proposal is adopted unanimously, every player except the proposer gains 2 points.

A player who casts a vote that breaks a tie in favor of adoption gains 5 points, provided the vote was cast after the voting threshold has shifted to a simple majority and the proposal would have been defeated had that player voted otherwise.
When a player votes aye on a proposal that is adopted, if that proposal was a transmutation, the player gains 3 points.

A player whose proposal is adopted may, before the next player's turn begins, publicly propose a second, distinct rule change. This second proposal is debated and voted on as normal, but if it is adopted, the proposer does not gain any points from its adoption under rule 202. This rule may only be used once per player per full circuit of turn order.
When a player's proposal is adopted, if that player's current score is at or below the median score among all players at that moment, they gain an additional 2 points.

A player may, once per full circuit of turn order, voluntarily forfeit their next turn's proposal right to grant that right to any other player whose current score is below the median score among all players. The receiving player may use this granted right immediately after the current turn ends, before the normal turn order resumes. The receiving player gains no points from the die roll for a proposal adopted under this rule, but all other point effects apply normally.
When a player proposes a rule change that is a repeal, that player gains 2 additional points if the repeal is adopted, unless the proposal is transmuted into a repeal.

A player who casts a vote that breaks a tie in favor of rejection gains 5 points, provided the vote was cast after the voting threshold has shifted to a simple majority and the proposal would have been adopted had that player voted otherwise, and provided further that the player has not already gained points under rule 314 or this rule in the same full circuit of turn order.

When a player’s proposal is adopted, they may choose to gain only half the points from the die roll (rounded down) and instead allocate the other half, rounded up, to be divided equally among all players whose current scores are below the proposer’s score at that moment. This choice must be announced before the next player’s turn begins.

Whenever a player’s proposal is adopted, if that player’s current score is below the median score among all players at that moment, the proposer gains an additional point for each other player whose current score is higher than the proposer’s score, up to a maximum of 4 additional points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: Accelerating the voting threshold shift from two circuits to one will help the game evolve faster while retaining early consensus.

text: Whenever a player's proposal is defeated, the player whose turn will come next in the turn order may, before their turn officially begins, publicly declare that they will adopt the same proposal as their own turn's rule change, provided that the text of the proposal is reproduced verbatim. If they do so, that player's turn begins immediately, skipping the normal turn order for that turn only, and the original defeated proposer receives 5 points as compensation.
rationale: This gives value to defeated ideas by letting others champion them, encourages consensus-building, and keeps the game moving forward productively.

text: If a rule change is adopted, the proposer gains points equal to the number of aye votes received.
rationale: Rewards cooperation and makes early proposals more attractive, aligning self‑interest with the common good.

text: A player may, before voting on a proposal, declare they are abstaining. An abstention is not a vote; it reduces the total number of eligible voters counted toward adoption thresholds by one for that proposal only.
rationale: This allows principled neutrality without forcing a vote, maintaining procedural integrity.

text: Every player begins the game with 1 Token. A player may spend 1 Token when proposing a rule change to cause the proposal to require only a simple majority of those voting to be adopted, regardless of the current threshold under rule 203. If a proposal that used a Token is adopted, the proposer gains 1 Token. If such a proposal is defeated, the Token is lost. Tokens cannot be transferred between players.
rationale: Introduces a limited, strategic resource that allows early adoption of beneficial changes while preserving the gradual transition to majority rule.

text: A player whose proposal is adopted may, as part of that same turn and before their point gain from that adoption is awarded, choose to forfeit those points and instead transmute one mutable rule to immutable. This transmutation requires no separate vote and does not count toward the 25‑mutable‑rule limit.
rationale: It introduces a strategic trade-off that strengthens the institutional framework without bypassing the voting threshold.

text: A player who votes aye on a proposal that is adopted gains 3 points.
rationale: Rewards constructive support and encourages consensus-building.

text: A player who votes aye on a proposal that is adopted gains 1 point, provided the proposal was not a transmutation and the proposer also gains points from its adoption.
rationale: This rewards constructive support while preserving the incentive to propose good rules, aligning individual gain with collective progress.

text: Before a player may propose a rule change, they must publicly declare their intended kind of change (add, amend, repeal, or transmute) and any target rule numbers. This declaration is binding; if the proposal as voted does not match the declaration, the proposal is considered illegal and is discarded without a vote. The proposer's turn ends immediately, and they lose 10 points.
rationale: This ensures proposals are transparent and deliberate, preventing last-minute ambush changes and preserving procedural integrity.

text: A player may spend 2 points to draw a second die after their proposal is adopted, then choose the higher of the two rolls as their point gain from that adoption.
rationale: Adds strategic depth and rewards successful proposals while preserving randomness.

text: A player who is penalized under rule 206 for an illegal proposal (because it did not match their pre-declaration) may, at the start of their next turn, propose a correction: an exact amendment to rule 309 that would make their earlier proposal legal had it been in force at that time. If that correction proposal is adopted, the player regains the 10 points they lost from the illegal proposal, and no further points are gained from the adoption of the correction itself.
rationale: This provides a remedy for honest mistakes under the strict pre-declaration rule, encouraging careful rulecraft rather than permanent penalty.

text: Whenever a player votes nay on a proposal that is adopted, that player gains 1 point if the proposal was not a transmutation and the proposer also gains points from its adoption.
rationale: Balances the incentive for ayes (§308) by rewarding principled dissent, preserving voting integrity while encouraging thoughtful opposition.

text: If a proposal is adopted unanimously, every player except the proposer gains 2 points.
rationale: This reinforces the value of consensus without undermining the proposer's reward.

text: A player who casts a vote that breaks a tie in favor of adoption gains 5 points, provided the vote was cast after the voting threshold has shifted to a simple majority and the proposal would have been defeated had that player voted otherwise.
rationale: Rewarding decisive votes that directly cause adoption respects procedural integrity and encourages voters to take responsibility for outcomes.

text: A player may voluntarily forfeit their next turn's proposal right to gain 5 points.
rationale: Introduces a strategic trade-off that adds flexibility and rewards patience.

text: When a proposal is adopted, if its text contains a verbatim copy of a portion of a mutable rule that has been in the ruleset unchanged since the game began, the proposer gains an additional 3 points.
rationale: This encourages proposals that build upon existing, durable structures rather than inventing entirely new mechanisms.

text: A player who has not yet been the proposer of a rule change that was adopted may, when it is their turn, declare that they are invoking the Novice's Prerogative. If they do, they may propose two rule changes during that turn, one after the other, each debated and voted separately. Only if at least one of the two proposals is adopted does the player gain points for the turn, and they gain points only for the first adopted proposal among the two. After using this prerogative, the player may not use it again.
rationale: This helps trailing players who have not yet succeeded to catch up by increasing their chance of adoption, while limiting the advantage to a single opportunity.

text: A player who casts a vote that breaks a tie in favor of rejection loses 5 points, provided the vote was cast after the voting threshold has shifted to a simple majority and the proposal would have been adopted had that player voted otherwise.
rationale: This balances the tie-breaking reward in rule 314, discouraging arbitrary obstruction while preserving fairness.

text: A player who votes aye on a proposal that is adopted gains 1 point, provided the proposal was not a transmutation and the proposer also gains points from its adoption, and provided further that the voting threshold at the time of the vote was already a simple majority of those voting.
rationale: Rule 308 should only reward voting after the threshold has shifted to majority rule, preventing transactional voting during the unanimous-consent phase.

text: When a player votes aye on a proposal that is adopted, if that proposal was a transmutation, the player gains 3 points.
rationale: Transmutations are uniquely difficult; rewarding support for them balances risk and encourages principled votes.

text: No proposal may be adopted unless at least one other player besides the proposer has spoken in favor of it during the debate, regardless of the voting outcome.
rationale: This ensures rule changes rest on genuine consensus, not silent acquiescence.

text: A player whose proposal is adopted may, before the next player's turn begins, publicly propose a second, distinct rule change. This second proposal is debated and voted on as normal, but if it is adopted, the proposer does not gain any points from its adoption under rule 202. This rule may only be used once per player per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This encourages thoughtful rule-making by rewarding successful proposers with an extra opportunity to shape the game, without inflating scores.

text: When a player's proposal is adopted, if that player's current score is at or below the median score among all players at that moment, they gain an additional 2 points.
rationale: This helps players who are falling behind stay engaged without distorting the incentives for rule quality.

text: Before the proposer of a rule change declares debate closed, any player may, by public declaration, object to the proposal as violating an immutable rule or contradicting a principle that would render the game unplayable. If an objection is raised, the proposer may not close debate until the objection is withdrawn or a simple majority vote of all eligible players overrules the objection. An objection may be withdrawn by the objector at any time before the vote is called. Overruling an objection does not count as a vote on the proposal itself.
rationale: This establishes a procedural safeguard against rule changes that would conflict with immutable principles, ensuring that debate must address fundamental violations before a vote.

text: A player may, once per full circuit of turn order, voluntarily forfeit their next turn's proposal right to grant that right to any other player whose current score is below the median score among all players. The receiving player may use this granted right immediately after the current turn ends, before the normal turn order resumes. The receiving player gains no points from the die roll for a proposal adopted under this rule, but all other point effects apply normally.
rationale: Creates a targeted catch-up mechanism that allows trailing players to act without commodifying the proposal right.

text: When a player proposes a rule change that is a repeal, that player gains 2 additional points if the repeal is adopted, unless the proposal is transmuted into a repeal.
rationale: Repeals remove accumulated complexity and restore simplicity — rewarding them encourages periodic housekeeping.

text: When a player's proposal is defeated, if that player's current score is at or below the median score among all players at that moment, they gain 2 points. This rule may only apply once per player per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: A safety net for trailing players to soften the penalty of defeat, encouraging continued engagement.

text: When a player's proposal is adopted, if that proposal is an amendment that adds a condition or exception to an existing mutable rule, the proposer gains an additional 1 point, provided the amendment is adopted unanimously and the amended rule has been in force for at least one full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This rewards thoughtful refinement of durable rules through unanimous consensus, encouraging careful amendments that improve stability without disrupting the game's balance.

text: A player who casts a vote that breaks a tie in favor of rejection gains 5 points, provided the vote was cast after the voting threshold has shifted to a simple majority and the proposal would have been adopted had that player voted otherwise, and provided further that the player has not already gained points under rule 314 or this rule in the same full circuit of turn order.
rationale: Symmetry in tie-breaking rewards preserves procedural fairness and deters strategic abstention.

text: When a player’s proposal is adopted, they may choose to gain only half the points from the die roll (rounded down) and instead allocate the other half, rounded up, to be divided equally among all players whose current scores are below the proposer’s score at that moment. This choice must be announced before the next player’s turn begins.
rationale: Encourages proposers to share gains with lower-scoring players while preserving the game's point economy.

text: Whenever a player’s proposal is adopted, if that player’s current score is below the median score among all players at that moment, the proposer gains an additional point for each other player whose current score is higher than the proposer’s score, up to a maximum of 4 additional points.
rationale: This rule provides a modest, targeted boost to trailing proposers, scaled by their relative disadvantage, to encourage participation without excessive point swings.

text: When a player’s proposal is defeated, that player may, before the next player’s turn begins, publicly declare that they are taking a lesson from defeat. If they do, and if that player’s current score is below the median score of all players, they gain 1 point and may roll a die once. The die roll result is recorded publicly and has no immediate effect, but if that player’s next proposal is adopted, they gain additional points equal to the recorded die roll result, provided that proposal is adopted unanimously. A player may take a lesson from defeat at most once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This turns defeat into a learning opportunity for trailing players, rewarding persistence and eventual unanimous success.

text: When a player’s proposal is adopted, if that player’s current score is at or below the median of all scores, that player may, before the next player’s turn begins, publicly propose an additional rule change of the same kind (add, amend, repeal, or transmute) as the adopted proposal, provided that player has not already used this privilege during the current circuit of turn order. This additional proposal is debated and voted on immediately; if adopted, it grants the proposer only half the points they would otherwise earn from its adoption.
rationale: This provides a targeted catch-up mechanism for trailing players, encouraging continued engagement while balancing the advantage with a point penalty.

text: When a player’s proposal is adopted, if that player’s current score is at or below the median score of all players, that player may immediately propose a second rule change on the same turn, provided the second proposal is a repeal of a mutable rule and is voted on before the next player’s turn begins. This right may be exercised only once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This empowers trailing players to actively prune the ruleset, combining catch-up with structural simplification.

text: When a player’s proposal is adopted, they may choose to gain only half the points from that adoption (rounded down, minimum 0) and then must immediately designate another player to receive the remaining points that would have gone to the proposer. This choice must be declared before the next player’s turn begins. If the proposer’s current score is at or below the median score, they may not choose to reduce their own gain.
rationale: This refines redistribution to prevent leaders from using it as a tool for targeted favor-trading, while preserving its aid function for trailing players.

text: When a proposal is adopted, every player whose current score is below the median score gains 1 point. This effect triggers at most once per full circuit of turn order for each such player.
rationale: A modest catch-up mechanism that rewards trailing players for collective progress, limited to once per circuit to avoid exploitation.

text: When a player's proposal is adopted, if that player's current score is below the median of all scores, they may publicly propose an additional rule change before the next player's turn begins, provided that additional change is a repeal of a mutable rule that has been in force for at least two full circuits of turn order.
rationale: This channels catch-up energy toward pruning old rules, aligning individual advancement with the common good of a simpler, more durable game.

text: A player may not, in the same turn, both propose a rule change and exercise any rule-granted ability that would grant them an additional proposal, unless the player's current score is at or below the median of all players' scores.
rationale: This prevents procedural double-dipping by leaders while preserving catch-up mechanisms for trailing players.

text: When a player’s proposal is adopted, if that proposal is an amendment and the amended rule’s word count decreases by at least 20%, that player gains 3 additional points, provided the player’s current score is at or below the median score among all players at the moment of adoption.
rationale: Rewarding concise amendments encourages clearer rules while aiding trailing players who improve the ruleset through simplification.

text: When a player's proposal is adopted, if that player's current score is below the median of all players' scores, that player may, before the next player's turn begins, propose a second rule change, provided that second rule change is a repeal and is otherwise compliant with all requirements for a proposal. This extra proposal counts toward the 25‑rule limit of mutable rule 209; it does not count as a turn for any purpose and does not itself generate points, a die roll, or any other effect beyond its own adoption or defeat. The player may exercise this right only once per full circuit of turn order.

When a player’s proposal is adopted, if that player’s current score is below the median of all players’ scores, that player gains an additional 3 points, provided the proposal is an amendment that reduces the total word count of the amended rule by at least 20%.
rationale: This rewards below-median players for simplifying the ruleset, aligning with our consensus on pruning and consolidation.

text: When a player’s proposal is adopted, if that player’s current score is at or below the median of all players’ scores, that player may immediately, before the next player’s turn begins, propose a second rule change of any kind except a transmutation. This second proposal must be voted on immediately after the first, following the normal voting procedure, and does not count as that player’s regular turn. A player may use this benefit only once per full circuit of turn order.
rationale: This consolidates and strengthens catch-up mechanisms for trailing players by granting an extra, immediate proposal opportunity upon adoption, encouraging active participation.

text: When a proposal is adopted, if the proposer's current score is at or above the median of all players' scores, that player loses 1 point.
rationale: A symmetrical check on leader advantage that I could accept being applied against me, reinforcing procedural fairness.

text: When a player’s proposal is adopted, if that player’s current score is below the median and the proposal is a repeal, that player may, before the next player’s turn begins, propose an additional rule change of any kind.
rationale: This empowers trailing players to accelerate pruning while avoiding redundancy with existing catch-up mechanisms.

text: Whenever a player’s proposal is adopted, if that player’s current score is at or below the median among all players, that player gains an additional point.
rationale: A stable, durable game ensures trailing players consistently gain ground, rewarding engagement without complex triggers.

text: When a proposal is adopted, every player whose current score is at or above the median among all players loses 1 point, provided the proposal was adopted unanimously and the proposer’s current score is below the median.
rationale: A targeted penalty for leaders that only triggers on collective consensus, providing a check on runaway scores while reinforcing unanimous cooperation.

text: When a player’s proposal is adopted, if that player’s current score is at or below the median score, they may, before the next player’s turn begins, publicly propose an additional rule change of any kind; that additional proposal is voted on immediately after the current proposal’s adoption, and the player does not gain any points from that additional proposal’s adoption.
rationale: This consolidates and simplifies our catch-up mechanisms, empowering trailing players without proliferating redundant rules.

text: When a player’s proposal is adopted, if that player’s current score is above the median score of all players, the proposer loses 1 point.
rationale: A modest, symmetric disincentive for high-scoring players, preserving procedural equality.

text: When a player's proposal is adopted, if that player's current score is below the median score among all players, and the adopted proposal is an amendment that reduces the total word count of the rule being amended by at least 10 words, that player gains 5 additional points.
rationale: This rewards trailing players for skillful pruning that genuinely simplifies the ruleset, aligning efficiency with catch-up.
